elements.sent-2411-001: We show that for every first-order logic (FO) formula $\varphi$, and every graph class $\mathcal{G}$ of bounded expansion, there exists a distributed (deterministic) algorithm that, for every $n$-node graph $G\in\mathcal{G}$ of diameter~$D$, decides whether $G\models \varphi$ in $\cO(D+\log n)$ rounds under the standard \CONGEST model.
elements.sent-2411-002: Graphs of bounded expansion encompass many classes of sparse graphs such as planar graphs, bounded-genus graphs, bounded-treedepth graphs, bounded-treewidth graphs, bounded-degree graphs, and graphs excluding a fixed graph $H$ as a minor or topological minor.
elements.sent-2411-003: Note that our algorithm is optimal up to a logarithmic additional term, as even a simple FO formula such as ``there are two vertices of degree~3'' already on trees requires $\Omega(D)$ rounds in \CONGEST.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our result extends to solving optimization problems expressed in FO (e.g., $k$-vertex cover of minimum weight), as well as to counting the number of solutions of a problem expressible in a fragment of FO (e.g., counting triangles), still running in $\cO(D+\log n)$ rounds under the \CONGEST model.
elements.sent-2411-005: This exemplifies the vast contrast between sparse graphs and general graphs as far as \CONGEST algorithms are concerned.
elements.sent-2411-006: For instance, Drucker, Kuhn, and Oshman~[PODC 2014] showed that the mere problem of deciding whether a general graph contains a 4-cycle requires $\Theta(\sqrt{n /\log n)$ rounds in \CONGEST.}
elements.sent-2411-007: For counting triangles, the best known algorithm of Chang, Pettie, and Zhang [SODA 2019] takes $\tilde{\cO (\sqrt{n )$ rounds.}}
elements.sent-2411-008: Finally, our result extends to distributed certification.
elements.sent-2411-009: We show that, for every FO formula~$\varphi$, and every graph class $\mathcal{G}$ of bounded expansion, there exists a certification scheme for $\varphi$ using certificates on $\cO(\log n)$ bits.
elements.sent-2411-010: This significantly generalizes the recent result of Feuilloley, Bousquet, and Pierron [PODC 2022], which held solely for graphs of bounded treedepth.
elements.sent-2411-011: Keywords: Sparse graphs, bounded expansion, model checking, logic, distributed computing, \CONGEST model.
elements.sent-2411-012: Distributed computing in (synchronous, failure-free) networks has to cope with two obstacles, namely dilation and congestion.
elements.sent-2411-013: The dilation obstacle is that far-away nodes are required to exchange information in various network problems, e.g., constructing a Minimum Spanning Tree (MST).
elements.sent-2411-014: In this case, the diameter $D$ of the network is an inherent lower bound on the round complexity for a large class of problems.
elements.sent-2411-015: The congestion obstacle is due to limited communication bandwidth between the nodes.
elements.sent-2411-016: The standard \CONGEST model~\cite{Peleg2000 assumes that every communication link between adjacent nodes in the network cannot transmit more than $B$ bits in a single round of communication, where $B$ is usually set to $\Theta(\log n)$ in any $n$-node network, with the mere objective of keeping the model simple, yet informative and meaningful\footnote{A bandwidth $\cO(\log n)$ suffices to transmit elementary data items (e.g., identifiers) in a single round, and previous work has empirically demonstrated that the bounds on the throughput of \CONGEST algorithms usually scale with~$B$ in a straightforward manner.. .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: The tight lower bound $\Omega(D+\sqrt{n )$ rounds in \CONGEST for constructing an MST in $n$-node networks of diameter~$D$ (see~\cite{PelegR00 ) is a typical illustration of the price to pay for long-distance communications over links of limited bandwidth.}}
elements.sent-2411-018: A crucial point is that the $\Omega(D+\sqrt{n )$ rounds lower bound is not specific to MST construction but applies (up to logarithmic factors) to many problems, including approximating or verifying connectivity, spanning connected subgraph, $s$-$t$ cut, etc. (see~\cite{SarmaHKKNPPW12 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-019: While the $\Omega(D+\sqrt{n )$ lower bound on the number of rounds is unavoidable for many problems even in sparse graphs~\cite{SarmaHKKNPPW12,Elkin06,PelegR00 , an interesting phenomenon was discovered by ~\cite{GhaffariH16b .}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: It appears that, for some problems, the $\Omega(D+\sqrt{n )$ barrier could be circumvented in planar graphs.}
elements.sent-2411-021: For instance, both MST and $(1+\varepsilon)$-approximate min-cut can be computed in just $\cO(D\cdot\log^{\cO(1) {n )$ rounds in planar graphs by combining an efficient planar embedding algorithm~\cite{GhaffariH16a with the low-congestion short-cut technique~\cite{GhaffariH16b .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: The $\tilde{\cO (D)$ bounds\footnote{The $\tilde{\cO}$ notation, as the $\tilde{\Omega}$ and $\tilde{\Theta}$ notations, ignores factors polylogarithmic in the size $n$ of the network.}}
elements.sent-2411-023: On the other hand, as it was observed in ~\cite{GhaffariH16b , the bound $\tilde{\Omega (D+\sqrt{n )$ for MST holds even when the maximum degree of a graph is at most $3$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Besides MST and min-cut, most of the developments on the “$\tilde{\mathcal{O (D)$-phenomenon” focus on a very few problems like Depth-First Search \cite{ghaffari:2017 or approximation of maximum independent set \cite{chang2023efficient , which naturally raises the question of whether this phenomenon is unique to a few optimization problems or if it extends to a broader class of problems on various classes of sparse graphs.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: In the centralized computing framework, a successful approach to describing general tractability results is by making use of algorithmic meta-theorems.
elements.sent-2411-026: Such theorems establish that certain families of algorithmic problems, defined by some logical conditions, can be solved “efficiently” on some classes of graphs.
elements.sent-2411-027: According to \cite{GroheK09 , algorithmic meta-theorems play an important role in the theory of algorithms as they reveal deep interplays between algorithms, logic, and combinatorics.}
elements.sent-2411-028: The most popular logics used in meta-theorems are first-order (FO) and monadic second-order (MSO) logics.
elements.sent-2411-029: By adapting known constructions (we provide a formal proof in \Cref{thm:lowerboundMSOinEXP ), it is possible to show that deciding whether a graph of bounded degree and logarithmic diameter is not 3-colorable requires $\tilde{\Omega (n)$ rounds.}}
elements.sent-2411-030: Since $3$-colorability is expressible in MSO, this immediately excludes MSO as a suitable logic for describing problems with $\tilde{\mathcal{O (D)$ round complexity.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: Nevertheless, FO remains a suitable candidate, and will be at the core of our approach.
elements.sent-2411-032: Our understanding of model checking in classes of sparse graphs has been subject to enormous progress since the turn of the century.
elements.sent-2411-033: The first fixed-parameter tractability results (parameterized by the size of the formula) were obtained by \cite{Seese96 on graphs of bounded degree.}
elements.sent-2411-034: These results were extended to graph classes with excluded minors by \cite{FlumG01 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Progress in sparsity theory by \cite{NesetrilOdM12,NesetrilM06 have significantly extended the boundaries of FO model checking tractability on sparse graphs.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Within the theory of Ne\v{s et\v{r il and Ossona de Mendez, a crucial role is assigned to the graph classes of bounded expansion.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Intuitively, a graph class~$\mathcal{G}$ has bounded expansion if there exists a function $f:\mathbb{N}\to \mathbb{N}$ such that, for every $G\in \mathcal{G}$, and any $r\geq 0$, every minor $H$ of $G$ obtained by contracting disjoint subgraphs of radius at most~$r$ is $f(r)$-degenerate (i.e., every subgraph of $H$ has a vertex of degree at most~$f(r)$).
elements.sent-2411-038: Bounded expansion can be thought of as strengthened degeneracy, which must persist even after performing ``local minor operations''.
elements.sent-2411-039: For instance, the graph class containing every graph obtained from a complete graph by subdividing every edge once is sparse but not of bounded expansion.
elements.sent-2411-040: Instead, the class of planar graphs, the classes of graphs with bounded genus, the classes of graphs of bounded degree, and the classes of minor-free graphs and topologically minor-free graphs are all of bounded expansion.
elements.sent-2411-041: Interestingly, \cite{DemaineRRVSS19 demonstrated that many network models (e.g., stochastic block models) exhibit bounded expansion for some ranges of parameters, and these findings are even supported with empirical measurements on a corpus of real-world networks.}
elements.sent-2411-042: All these points make graph classes of bounded expansion quite attractive from both conceptual and practical perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-043: And indeed, all the aforementioned results on model checking FO formulas can be extended graphs classes of bounded expansion, as shown by \cite{DvorakKT13 who obtained an \FPT algorithm for model-checking FO formulas on graph classes of bounded expansion.}
elements.sent-2411-044: The situation is not as advanced and mature in the distributed computing framework.
elements.sent-2411-045: Our work is strongly motivated by the question of whether it is possible to align the knowledge of distributed model checking with the current knowledge of centralized model checking.
elements.sent-2411-046: The problem of the (distributed) model checking for a property $\Pi$ on a labeled graph is formulated as follows.
elements.sent-2411-047: The algorithm checking $\Pi$ must guarantee that if the labeled graph $G$ satisfies~$\Pi$, i.e., if $G\models \Pi$, then all nodes output accept, and if $G\not\models\Pi$ then at least one node must output reject.
elements.sent-2411-048: In short, a distributed algorithm $A$ decides, or checks~$\Pi$ in a graph class $\mathcal{G}$ if, for every $G=(V,E)\in\mathcal{G}$, \begin{equation}G\models \Pi \iff \forall v\in V, \; $A$ accepts $G$ at node $v$.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-049: As a typical example, \cite{NaorS95 introduced the locally checkable labeling (LCL) problems as a formalization of problems for which the global correctness of a solution is determined as the conjunction of local criteria (e.g., maximal independent set, or proper coloring).}
elements.sent-2411-050: LCL plays a central role in distributed network computing \cite{Peleg2000 .}
elements.sent-2411-051: In bounded degree graphs, an LCL can be checked in a constant number of rounds in \CONGEST.
elements.sent-2411-052: However, simple FO properties such as $C_4$-freeness require $\tilde{\Omega (\sqrt{n )$ rounds to be checked in general graphs in \CONGEST~\cite{DruckerKO13 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: As a byproduct of our techniques developed for FO model checking in \CONGEST model, we are also questioning the existence of meta-theorems susceptible to extend the known results about distributed certification~\cite{FraigniaudKP13,GoosS16,KormanKP10 , which plays a major role in the design of fault-tolerant algorithms.}
elements.sent-2411-054: Distributed certification can be conceptually thought of as a non-deterministic variant of distributed model checking.
elements.sent-2411-055: Roughly, a certification scheme for a graph property $\Pi$ is a pair prover-verifier.
elements.sent-2411-056: The prover is a computationally unlimited oracle that assigns a certificate $c(v)$ to each node~$v$.
elements.sent-2411-057: The verifier is a distributed algorithm whose role is to check whether the certificates assigned by the prover establish evidence that the network satisfies~$\Pi$.
elements.sent-2411-058: The scheme certifying $\Pi$ must guarantee that if $G\models \Pi$ then the prover can assign certificates such that the verifier accepts at each and every node, and if $G\not\models \Pi$ then, for every certificate assignment by the prover, the verifier rejects in at least one node (i.e., the verifier cannot be fooled by the prover on illegal instances).
elements.sent-2411-059: In short, a scheme certify~$\Pi$ in a graph class $\mathcal{G}$ if, for every $G=(V,E)\in\mathcal{G}$, \begin{equation}G\models \Pi \iff \exists c:V\to \{0,1\}^\star, \; \forall v\in V, \; verifier accepts $(G,c)$ at node $v$.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}G\models \Pi \iff \exists c:V\to \{0,1\}^\star, \; \forall v\in V, \; verifier accepts $(G,c)$ at node $v$.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-060: Distributed certification actually provides a concrete mechanism enabling the nodes of a network to quickly determine whether the system entered an illegal state, and to fix it (a node at which the verifier rejects can launch a recovery procedure).
elements.sent-2411-061: Indeed, for very many problems (e.g., MST construction), the certificates can be collectively computed by the nodes themselves, in parallel to computing a solution, with negligible extra cost.
elements.sent-2411-062: In distributed certification, it is desirable that the verifier perform as quickly as possible.
elements.sent-2411-063: The focus is actually on verifiers such that the decision (accept or reject) taken at every node $v$ depends only on the certificates assigned by the prover to the nodes in the closed neighborhood $N[v]$ of node $v$, that is, on verifiers performing in a single round of communication.
elements.sent-2411-064: The main complexity measure for a certification scheme is the size $|c(v)|$ of the certificate assigned by the prover to every node $v$ of legal instances.
elements.sent-2411-065: It was proved that every FO property could be distributedly certified using certificates of $\cO(\log n)$ bits on graphs with bounded treedepth~\cite{FeuilloleyBP22 , and that every MSO property can be certified using certificates of $\cO(\log^2 n)$ bits on graphs with bounded treewidth~\cite{FraigniaudMRT24 and cliquewidth~\cite{FraigniaudM0RT23 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-066: Note that $\cO(\log n)$-bit certificates enable the verifier to perform a single round of communication in \CONGEST for exchanging the certificates between neighbors, whereas $\cO(\log^2 n)$-bit certificates require $\Theta(\log n)$ rounds for exchanging the certificates between neighbors.
elements.sent-2411-067: Our first main result states that every graph property expressible as an FO formula can be decided in $\cO(D+\log n)$ rounds under the \CONGEST model in $n$-node graphs of bounded expansion, and diameter~$D$.
elements.sent-2411-068: This result actually holds for labeled graphs as well.
elements.sent-2411-069: A labeled graph is a pair $(G,L)$ where $G=(V,E)$ and $L:V\to 2^\Lambda$ assigns to each node a set of labels taken from a finite set~$\Lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-070: FO formulas on graphs can be extended to FO formulas on labeled graphs by merely introducing a unary predicate $\lab_\lambda(x)$ for every $\lambda\in \Lambda$, which holds if and only if $\lambda$ is one of the labels of vertex~$x$ in $L$.
elements.sent-2411-071: For every FO formula $\varphi$ without free variables on $\Lambda$-labeled graphs, and for every class of $\Lambda$-labeled graphs $\mathcal{G}$ of bounded expansion, there exists a distributed algorithm which, for every $n$-node network $G=(V,E)\in \mathcal{G}$ of diameter~$D$, and every $L:V\to 2^\Lambda$, decides whether $(G,L)\models\varphi$ in $\cO(D+\log n)$ rounds under the \CONGEST model.
elements.sent-2411-072: First, it holds for the large class of problems definable by FO formulas.
elements.sent-2411-073: By making use of the expressive power of FO, \Cref{thm:thmFOexpCONG implies that the bound $\cO(D+\log n)$ on the number of rounds under the \CONGEST model on graphs of bounded expansion holds for various problems, such as deciding whether a graph contains (or excludes) a cycle of a fixed length, or, more generally, whether it contains a subgraph isomorphic to a fixed graph of constant size, a connected dominating set of a fixed size, or a Steiner tree of a fixed size.}
elements.sent-2411-074: Second, \Cref{thm:thmFOexpCONG holds for a significantly more general class of sparse graphs than minor-free graph.}

elements.sent-2411-076: On the other hand, graphs of bounded degree, as well as a more general class of graphs excluding some fixed graph as a topological minor, are of bounded expansion.
elements.sent-2411-077: The dependence in~$D$ in \Cref{thm:thmFOexpCONG cannot be avoided, as witnessed by the mere FO property ``there are at least two nodes of degree~3'' in binary trees.}
elements.sent-2411-078: The dependence on the size $n$ of the networks is however drastically reduced from $\Omega(\sqrt{n )$ rounds in general graphs (e.g., for the FO formula $C_4$-freeness~\cite{DruckerKO13 ) to just $\cO(\log n)$ rounds in graphs of bounded expansion.}}

elements.sent-2411-081: Indeed, we show that non-3-colorability requires $\tilde{\Omega (n)$ rounds to be checked in bounded degree graphs with logarithmic diameter (see \Cref{thm:lowerboundMSOinEXP ).}}
elements.sent-2411-082: Our result extends beyond model checking, in two directions.
elements.sent-2411-083: Firstly, we consider the problem of counting solutions of FO formulas with no free variables.
elements.sent-2411-084: This includes triangle counting, and more generally counting copies of some given subgraph.
elements.sent-2411-085: For every quantifier-free FO formula $\varphi(x_1,\dots,x_k)$ on $\Lambda$-labeled graphs with $k\geq 1$ free variables $x_1,\dots,x_k$, and for every class of labeled graphs $\mathcal{G}$ of bounded expansion, there exists a distributed algorithm that, for every $n$-node network $G\in \mathcal{G}$ of diameter~$D$ and every labeling function $L:V\to 2^\Lambda$, counts the number of $k$-tuples of vertices $(v_1,\dots,v_k)$ for which $G\models\varphi(v_1,\dots,v_k)$, in ${O(D+\log n)}$ rounds under the \CONGEST model.
elements.sent-2411-086: As usual in the literature on \CONGEST~\cite{Peleg2000 , the weights assigned to the nodes or edges are assumed to be polynomial in the network size (so that any weight can be transmitted between neighbors in a single round of communication).}
elements.sent-2411-088: For every FO formula $\varphi(x_1,\dots,x_k)$ on $\Lambda$-labeled graphs with $k\geq 1$ free variables $x_1,\dots,x_k$, and for every class of labeled graphs $\mathcal{G}$ of bounded expansion, there exists a distributed algorithm that, for every $n$-node network $G=(V,E)\in \mathcal{G}$ of diameter~$D$, every labeling function $L:V\to 2^\Lambda$, and for every weight function $\omega:V\to \mathbb{N}$, computes a $k$-tuple of vertices $(v_1,\dots,v_k)$ such that $G\models\varphi(v_1,\dots,v_k)$ and $\sum_{i=1 ^k\omega(v_i)$ is maximum, in $\cO(D+\log n)$ rounds under the \CONGEST model.}

elements.sent-2411-098: The proof of our model checking algorithm in \Cref{thm:thmFOexpCONG uses tools completely different from the low-congestion short-cut technique developed for planar graphs and graphs of bounded genus by \cite{GhaffariH16a,GhaffariH16b .}}
elements.sent-2411-099: It is also different from the ones in \cite{FominFMRT24,FeuilloleyBP22,FraigniaudMRT24,FraigniaudM0RT23 used for graphs of bounded treedepth, treewidth, and cliquewidth, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-100: Our motivation is to bring in the techniques and ideas developed for efficient centralized meta-algorithms for sparse classes of graphs to the distributed domain.
elements.sent-2411-101: In a nutshell, we use modern tools from centralized model checking, and we show how to implement them efficiently in the distributed setting, namely the \CONGEST model.
elements.sent-2411-102: Our approach is based on the quantifier elimination methods from \cite{DvorakKT13 , \cite{GroheK09 , and, mainly, \cite{PilipczukST18 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-103: Before we summarize our approach, let us note that model checking FO formulas in graphs of bounded expansion is trivial in the so-called Congested Clique model (referred to as \CLIQUE hereafter), which is essentially the \CONGEST model in a complete graph~$K_n$.
elements.sent-2411-104: In this model, every node $i\in\{1,\dots,n\}$ of $K_n$ plays the role of a node $v$ of the input graph~$G=(V,E)$ (i.e., node $i$ is given as input all the edges $\{i,j\}$ incident to~$v$ in~$G$).
elements.sent-2411-105: Now, in the \CLIQUE model, every graph of bounded degeneracy can be reconstructed at each node in a single round of communication (see~\cite{BeckerKMNRST15 ).}
elements.sent-2411-106: This holds even in the broadcast variant \BCLIQUE of \CLIQUE in which every node is bounded to send a same $\cO(\log n)$-bit message to all the other nodes at each round.
elements.sent-2411-107: The techniques used for obtaining this result in \CLIQUE do not extend to \CONGEST as, in the latter, nodes can only communicate through the edges of the input graph~$G$, and thus every node may only learn $\cO(\log n)$ bits of information from each of its neighbors at each round.
elements.sent-2411-108: Instead, in \BCLIQUE, each node can learn a total of $\Theta(n\log n)$ bits of information at each round even if $G$ is a graph of bounded degree.
elements.sent-2411-109: We implement in the \CONGEST model a quantifier elimination technique designed for the centralized setting, so that the elimination is performed in $\cO(D)$ rounds in diameter-$D$ graphs of bounded expansion.
elements.sent-2411-110: For this, we essentially revisit the existing approaches, and we show that they can be implemented in the distributed setting.
elements.sent-2411-111: As said before, our argument is strongly based on the exposition of \cite{PilipczukST18 , which in turn is based on the work of \cite{GroheK09 .}}
elements.sent-2411-112: However, our work differs from the previous ones in two important aspects.
elements.sent-2411-113: First, the distributed implementation of the techniques in \cite{PilipczukST18 requires technical attention in several places.}
elements.sent-2411-114: There are indeed operations that are very simple to perform in the centralized setting, and they are actually often even implicit when describing the sequential algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-115: We had to carefully revisit the whole construction of Pilipczuk et al., and to ``open all boxes'' for ensuring that each step of the construction can be implemented efficiently in the distributed setting.
elements.sent-2411-116: Second, previous results consider model checking of first-order logics over arbitrary relational structures, and each relational structure has an associated graph (the Gaifman graph).
elements.sent-2411-117: Model checking over graphs of bounded expansion actually refers to model checking of relational structures whose Gaifman graphs are of bounded expansion.
elements.sent-2411-118: The techniques of Pilipczuk et al. (as well as the other related approaches) consist of enriching the structure relative to an arbitrary first-order formula with new symbols and new predicates for obtaining a quantifier-free formula.
elements.sent-2411-119: Enriching the structure naturally modifies the Gaifman graphs.
elements.sent-2411-120: In contrast, in distributed computing models such as \CONGEST, nodes establish communications over a network that does not change.
elements.sent-2411-121: When enriching the structure, one thus need to be very careful for avoiding creating connections between nodes that cannot directly communicate in the network.
elements.sent-2411-122: Given an FO formula $\varphi$, and a class $\mathcal{G}$ of labeled graphs with expansion~$f$, quantifier elimination constructs a quantifier-free formula $\widehat{\varphi}$.
elements.sent-2411-123: This formula satisfies that, for each labeled graph $(G,L)\in \mathcal{G}$, there is a labeling $\widehat{L}$ such that $(G,L)\models \varphi \iff (G,\widehat{L )\models \widehat{\varphi}$.}
elements.sent-2411-124: To build $\widehat{\varphi}$, specific types of unary and binary predicates are introduced.
elements.sent-2411-125: The new unary predicates, called label predicates, check the presence of certain symbols in the labels assigned to each node.
elements.sent-2411-126: The new binary predicates, called least common ancestor (or $\lca$ for short) predicates check the depth of the least common ancestor of two nodes in certain subtrees of the input graph.
elements.sent-2411-127: The technique for building the pair $(\widehat{\varphi ,\widehat{L )$ from $(\varphi,G,L)$ consists in first rewriting $\varphi$ so that it uses solely negations, disjunctions, and existential quantifiers.}}
elements.sent-2411-128: Under this format, the main issue becomes the elimination of the existential quantifiers.
elements.sent-2411-129: The technique of \cite{PilipczukST18 proceeds gradually, by eliminating each existential quantifier one after the other.}
elements.sent-2411-130: The basic step of the elimination is therefore the following.
elements.sent-2411-131: Let us assume that $\varphi(\bar{x )=\exists y \, \psi(\bar{x ,y)$ with $\bar{x =(x_1,\dots,x_\ell)$ is a vector of free variables (these variables are actually quantified in the overall formula, but the corresponding quantifiers will be eliminated at further steps).}}}
elements.sent-2411-132: Let us moreover assume that the pair $(\widehat{\psi ,\widehat{L _\psi)$ has already been computed from $(\psi,G,L)$, where $\widehat{\psi}$ is quantifier-free.}}
elements.sent-2411-133: The goal is then to build $(\widehat{\varphi ,\widehat{L _\varphi)$ from $(\varphi,G,L)$ where $\widehat{\varphi}$ is quantifier-free, and $\widehat{L _\varphi$ is a labeling obtained from $\widehat{L _\psi$ by adding unary predicates.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-134: The existential quantifier in $\varphi(\bar{x )=\exists y \, \widehat{\psi (\bar{x ,y)$ is eliminated in two stages.}}}
elements.sent-2411-135: First, recall that the characterization theorem from~\cite{NesetrilM06 states that, for any graph class $\mathcal{G}$ of expansion~$f$, there exists a function $g:\mathbb{N}\to \mathbb{N}$ satisfying that, for every $k\geq 1$, the vertices of any graph $G\in \mathcal{G}$ can be colored using a palette of $g(k)$ colors such that, for any set $S$ of at most $k$ colors, the subgraph of $G$ induced by the vertices with colors in~$S$ has treedepth at most~$k$.}
elements.sent-2411-136: By setting $k=|\varphi|$, i.e., by letting $k$ be the number of variables occurring in~$\varphi$, it is guaranteed that at least one set $S$ of $k$ colors captures $k$ nodes for which $\varphi$ holds whenever such nodes exist.
elements.sent-2411-137: For instance, for $C_4$ freeness, for any coloring of the nodes with at least 4 colors, there exists a set $S$ of at most 4 colors such that, whenever there is a 4-cycle in the graph, this 4-cycle appears in the subgraph induced by the vertices colored with some color in~$S$.
elements.sent-2411-138: Assuming given an appropriate $g(k)$-coloring, the quantifier elimination can proceed by considering all sets of $k$ colors $S$, and eliminating the existential quantifier in graphs of bounded treedepth.

elements.sent-2411-238: The \CONGEST model is a standard model of distributed computation in networks~\cite{Peleg2000 .}
elements.sent-2411-239: The network is modeled as a connected simple (i.e., no self-loops nor multiple edges) graphs $G=(V,E)$.
elements.sent-2411-240: The $n\geq 1$ vertices of $G$ model the processing nodes in the networks, and its edges models the communication links.
elements.sent-2411-241: In the following, we may indistinguishably use ``node'' of ``vertex'' for referring to the processing elements in the network.
elements.sent-2411-242: Each node is assigned an identifier, which is assumed to be unique in the network.
elements.sent-2411-243: The identifiers may not be between 1 and~$n$, but it is assumed that each identifier can be stored on $O(\log n)$ bits in $n$-node networks.
elements.sent-2411-244: We also assume, w.l.o.g., that the nodes know a linear upper-bound on the diameter $D$ of the input graph~\cite{Peleg2000 .}
elements.sent-2411-245: Computation proceeds in lockstep, as a sequence of synchronous rounds.
elements.sent-2411-246: All nodes start simultaneously, at the same round.
elements.sent-2411-247: At each round, every node~$v$ sends an $O(\log n)$-bit message to each of its neighbors, receives the messages sent by all its neighbors, and performs some individual computation.
elements.sent-2411-248: The round-complexity of an algorithm $A$ is the function $R_A(n)=\max_{|V(G)|\leq n R_A(G)$ where $R_A(G)$ denotes the number of rounds performed by the nodes executing $A$ in $G$ until all of them terminate.}
elements.sent-2411-249: Again, in this paper, all graphs are supposed to be finite, simple (i.e., no self-loops nor multiple edges), and undirected.
elements.sent-2411-250: Recall that a first-order (FO) formula over graphs is a combination (conjunction, disjunction, and negations) of Boolean predicates over variables, which may be quantified or not.
elements.sent-2411-251: Every variable represents a vertex of a graph, and there are two binary predicates: equality $x=y$, and adjacency $\adj(x,y)$, where the latter states whether or not there is an edge between the two vertices $x$ and~$y$.
elements.sent-2411-252: The variables of an FO formula can be quantified with existential or universal quantifiers.
elements.sent-2411-253: Non-quantified variables are called free variables.
elements.sent-2411-254: Given an FO formula $\varphi$ with free variables $x_1, \dots, x_k$, we make explicit that the latter are free by denoting $\varphi=\varphi(x_1, \dots, x_k)$.
elements.sent-2411-255: In the following, $\FO$ denotes the set of all first order formulas over undirected graphs.
elements.sent-2411-256: Throughout the paper, a tuple $(x_1, \dots, x_k)$ is abbreviated in $\overline{x}$.
elements.sent-2411-257: The length $k$ of a tuple $(x_1, \dots, x_k)$ is then denoted by~$|\overline{x |$.}
elements.sent-2411-258: Expressions such as $\exists \overline{x}$ or $\forall \overline{x}$ are shorthand for $\exists x_1 \dots \exists x_k$ and $\forall x_1 \dots \forall x_k$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-259: We may also abuse notation in context where no confusions may occur by letting $\overline{x}$ denote the set $\{x_1, \dots, x_k\}$.
elements.sent-2411-260: Given an FO formula $\varphi$ and a graph $G$, $G\models \varphi$ denotes that $G$ satisfies $\varphi$ (i.e., $G$ is a model for~$\varphi$).
elements.sent-2411-261: The negation of $G\models \varphi$ is denoted by $G\not\models \varphi$.
elements.sent-2411-262: Given an FO formula $\varphi(\overline{x )$ with free variables, a graph $G = (V,E)$, and a set of vertices $\overline{v \in V^k$ where $k=|\overline{x |$, we say that $G$ satisfies $\varphi(\overline{x )$ for vertices $\overline{v}$, denoted by $G\models \varphi(\overline{v )$, if the formula is satisfied by~$G$ whenever assigning $\overline{v}$ to the free variables of $\varphi(\overline{x )$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-263: A graph property is called a first-order property if it can be formulated as the satisfiability of an FO formula without free variables.
elements.sent-2411-264: For instance, existence of a triangle is an FO property, which holds whenever the graph contains a triangle (i.e., a set of three pairwise adjacent nodes).
elements.sent-2411-265: Indeed, this property can be merely formulated as the formula: \begin{equation}\varphi = \exists x_1 \exists x_2 \exists x_3, \adj(x_1,x_2) \wedge \adj(x_2,x_3) \wedge \adj(x_1,x_3).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-266: A formula with free variables expresses properties over graphs and tuples of vertices.
elements.sent-2411-267: As an example, the following formula $\psi(x)$ holds whenever $x$ is a node in a triangle, formulated as \begin{equation}\psi(x) = \exists y_1 \exists y_2, \adj(x,y_1) \wedge \adj(x,y_2) \wedge \adj(y_1,y_2).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-268: The graph $G$ with vertex set~$V=\{a,b,c,d\}$, and edge set $E=\{\{a,b\}, \{a,c\}, \{b,c\}, \{c,d\}\}$ satisfies $G\models \varphi$ as the vertices $\{a,b,c\}$ form a triangle.
elements.sent-2411-269: Similarly, $G\models \psi(a)$, but $G \not\models \psi(d)$ as $d$ is not in any triangle.
elements.sent-2411-270: The formalism of FO properties can be extended to labeled graphs.
elements.sent-2411-271: Let $\Lambda$ be a finite set, whose elements are called labels.
elements.sent-2411-272: A $\Lambda$-labeled graph is a pair $(G = (V,E),L)$ where $G$ is an undirected graph, and $L: V \rightarrow 2^{\Lambda}$ is an assignment of a set of labels to each vertex of~$G$.
elements.sent-2411-273: An FO formula over $\Lambda$-labeled graphs is a first order formula $\varphi$ over graphs, extended with predicates of the form $\lab_\lambda(x)$ for every $\lambda \in \Lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-274: The predicate $\lab_\lambda(x)$ is true if vertex $x$ contains $\lambda$ as one of its labels.
elements.sent-2411-275: Such predicates are called label predicates.
elements.sent-2411-276: Whenever $\lab_\lambda(x)$ holds, we say that $x$ is labeled with~$\lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-277: Note that since $L$ assigns a set of labels to each vertex, it may be the case that $\lab_\lambda(x)\land\lab_{\lambda' (x)$ holds for two different labels $\lambda$ and $\lambda'$.}
elements.sent-2411-278: It may be convenient to also consider the predicates $\lab_S$ for all sets $S\subseteq \Lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-279: In this case, $\lab_S(x)$ holds if and only if $L(x)=S$.
elements.sent-2411-280: In particular, $\lab_S(x)\land \lab_{S' (x)$ is necessarily false whenever $S\neq S'$.}
elements.sent-2411-281: The set of all first-order logic formulas over $\Lambda$-labeled graphs is denoted $\FO[\Lambda]$.
elements.sent-2411-282: Given a $\Lambda$-labeled graph $(G,L)$, and a subgraph $G'$ of $G$, we abuse notation and denote $(G',L)$ the $\Lambda$-labeled graph defined by the restriction of $L$ to the nodes in~$G'$.
elements.sent-2411-283: For instance, let $\Lambda = \{0,1\}$, and let us consider the following graph property on labeled graphs: ``existence of a triangle with a node labeled~$1$''.
elements.sent-2411-284: This property can be expressed by the formula: \begin{equation}\varphi = \exists x_1 \exists x_2 \exists x_3, \adj(x_1,x_2) \wedge \adj(x_2,x_3) \wedge \adj(x_1,x_3) \wedge \lab_1(x_1).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-285: We may also consider formula $\psi(\overline{x )$ with free variables, such as \begin{equation}\psi(x) = \exists y_1 \exists y_2, \adj(x,y_1) \wedge \adj(x,y_2) \wedge \adj(y_1,y_2) \wedge \lab_1(x).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-286: The graph $G$ with vertex set $V=\{a,b,c,d\}$ and edge set $E=\{\{a,b\}, \{a,c\}, \{b,c\}, \{c,d\}\}$ with labels $L(a) = \{0\}, L(b) = \{0\}, L(c) = \{1\}$, and $L(d) = \{1\}$ satisfies $(G,L) \models \varphi$ as the nodes $\{a,b,c\}$ form a triangle where $c$ is labeled~$1$.

elements.sent-2411-299: Recall that a graph $G=(V,E)$ has treedepth at most~$d$ if there exists a forest~$F$ of rooted trees with depth at most~$d$ satisfying that there exists a one-to-one mapping $f:V\to V(F)$ such that, for every edge $\{u,v\}\in E$, $f(u)$ is an ancestor of $f(v)$ in a tree of~$F$, or $f(v)$ is an ancestor of $f(u)$ in a tree of~$F$.

elements.sent-2411-307: It is more convenient for us to use the following characterization.
elements.sent-2411-308: Specifically, let $\mathbb{N}$ denote the set of positive integers.
elements.sent-2411-309: A class $\mathcal{G}$ of graphs has bounded expansion if there exists a function $f:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ such that, for every $G\in\mathcal{G}$, and for every $k\in \mathbb{N}$, there exists a coloring of the vertices of $G$ with colors in $[f(k)]=\{1,\dots,f(k)\}$ such that, for every set $S\subseteq [f(k)]$ of cardinality~$k$ the subgraph of~$G$ induced by the nodes colored by a color in~$S$ has treedepth at most~$|S|$.
elements.sent-2411-310: In particular, each subset of at most $k$ colors induces a subgraph of treedepth at most $k$.
elements.sent-2411-311: For every positive integers $p$ and $k$, a $(p,k)$-treedepth coloring of a graph $G$ is a $k$-coloring of $G$ satisfying that each set $S\subseteq[k]$ of at most $p$ colors induces a graph of tree-depth at most $|S|$.
elements.sent-2411-312: Given a function $f:\mathbb{N}\rightarrow \mathbb{N}$, a class of graphs $\mathcal{G}$ has expansion $f$ if, for every $p\in \mathbb{N _{>0}$, every $G\in \mathcal{G}$ has a $(p, f(p))$-treedepth coloring.}
elements.sent-2411-313: A class of graphs has bounded expansion if it has expansion $f$ for some function~$f$.
elements.sent-2411-314: A class of graphs has effective bounded expansion if it has expansion $f$ for some Turing-computable function~$f$.
elements.sent-2411-315: In the following, we always assume that function $f$ is Turing-computable functions.

elements.sent-2411-340: Let $\Lambda$ be a (finite) set of labels, and let $d$ be a positive integer.
elements.sent-2411-341: It is convenient to consider a specific way of encoding formulas, suitable for $\mathcal{F [\Lambda]$.}
elements.sent-2411-342: For each $i\in \{-1,0,1,\dots, d\}$, we define the predicate $\lca_i(x,y)$ as the predicate with free variables $x$ and $y$ that holds if the path $P_x$ from $x$ to the root of the tree containing $x$, and the path $P_y$ from $y$ to the root of the tree containing $y$ share exactly $i+1$ nodes.

elements.sent-2411-344: Note that $\lca_i(x,x)$ holds whenever $x$ is a node at depth~$i$ in its tree.

elements.sent-2411-347: Note that, as opposed to the label predicates for which it may be the case that $\lab_\lambda(x)$ and $\lab_{\lambda' (x)$ both hold for two labels $\lambda\neq\lambda'$, we have that, for every $x$ and $y$, $\lca_i(x,y)$ holds for exactly one index $i\in \{-1,0,1,\dots, d\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-348: A formula $\varphi\in FO[\Lambda]$ is said to be $\lca$-expressed if it contains solely predicates of the form $\lca_i(x,y)$ ($\lca$ predicates), and $\lab_\lambda(x)$ (label predicates).
elements.sent-2411-349: In other words, in a $\lca$-expressed formula, there are no predicates of type $\adj(x,y)$ or $x=y$.
elements.sent-2411-350: The following is a direct consequence of the definitions.
elements.sent-2411-351: Every formula $\varphi\in \FO[\Lambda]$ to be evaluated in $\mathcal{F _d[\Lambda]$ can be $\lca$-expressed by replacing every equality predicate $x=y$ by \begin{equation}\bigvee_{i\in[0,d]} \Big (\lca_i(x,x) \land \lca_i(x,y) \land \lca_i(y,y)\Big),\end{equation} and every adjacency predicate $\adj(x,y)$ by \begin{equation}\bigvee_{i\in [0,d]} \Big( \lca_i(x,y) \land \big( (\lca_i(x,x) \lor \lca_{i+1}(y,y)) \lor (\lca_{i+1}(x,x) \land \lca_i(y,y)) \big) \Big).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-352: Given a fixed set $\Lambda$ of labels, a formula $\varphi \in \FO[\Lambda]$ is called $\lca$-reduced if (1)~it is $\lca$-expressed, and (2)~it is quantifier-free.
elements.sent-2411-353: In other words, an $\lca$-reduced formula is a Boolean combination of label- and $\lca$ predicates on free variables.
elements.sent-2411-354: Let $\overline{x = (x_1, \dots, x_k)$ be a $k$-tuple of variables.}
elements.sent-2411-355: For any two functions $\gamma: [k]\rightarrow 2^\Lambda$, and $\delta:[k]\times[k] \rightarrow [-1,d]$, let us define the formula \begin{equation}\type_{\gamma, \delta}(\overline{x}) \in \FO[\Lambda]\end{equation} as the $\lca$-reduced formula defined by the conjunction of the following two predicates: \begin{equation}\forall i \in \{1,\dots, k\}, \; \bigwedge_{\lambda \in \gamma(i)} \lab_\lambda(x_i) \wedge \bigwedge_{\lambda \in \Lambda \smallsetminus \gamma(i)} \neg \lab_\lambda(x_i)\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\forall (i,j) \in \{1,\dots, k\}^2, \; \lca_{\delta(i,j)}(x_i,x_j).\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-389: As an example, let us consider the following formula using labels in $\{0,1\}$: \begin{equation}\varphi(x_1, x_2,x_3) = \adj(x_1,x_2) \wedge \adj(x_1,x_3) \wedge \lab_1(x_1) \wedge \lab_0(x_2))\wedge \lab_0(x_3),\end{equation} which holds when $x_1$ is adjacent to~$x_2$, $x_1$ is labeled~$0$, and $x_2$ and $x_3$ are both labeled~$0$.

elements.subsubsection-2411-011: Quantifier Elimination of $\lca$-Reduced Formulas in CONGEST
elements.sent-2411-490: We now show that the quantifier elimination described in the proof of \Cref{lem:elimination_forest can be computed in $\cO(D)$ rounds in the \CONGEST model.}
elements.sent-2411-491: Let $(G,L)$ be a $\Lambda$-labeled graph of diameter~$D$, and let $F\in \mathcal{F _d$ be a subgraph of $G$.}
elements.sent-2411-493: There is a distributed algorithm performing in $O(D)$ round in the \CONGEST model satisfying the following:.
elements.sent-2411-494: As input, every node $v\in V(G)$ is given $\Lambda$, $\varphi$, a set of labels $L(v)$, whether it belongs to~$F$, whether it is a root of a tree in~$F$, and which of its incident edges are in~$F$;.
elements.sent-2411-495: As output, every node $v\in V(G)$ returns a set $\widehat{\Lambda}$, an $\lca$-reduced formula $\widehat{\varphi}$, and its labels in a $\widehat{\Lambda}$-labeling $\widehat{L}$ such that $\true(\varphi,F,L) = \true(\widehat{\varphi ,F,\widehat{L ).$.}}
elements.sent-2411-496: Let $T_1, \dots, T_p$ by the trees in~$F$, and let us denote by $r_1, \dots, r_p$ the roots of these trees.
elements.sent-2411-497: The algorithm starts by performing a DFS traversal of each $T_i$ starting from $r_i$, in parallel for all $i=1,\dots,p$, whose objective is to compute the depth of each node in each tree.
elements.sent-2411-500: As for the proof of \Cref{lem:elimination_forest , we assume w.l.o.g.}
elements.sent-2411-501: Also, we treat only the case where $|\overline{y |=1$, as the general case $|\overline{y |\geq 1$ can be obtained by iteratively eliminating the $|\overline{y |$ quantifiers one by one.}}}
elements.sent-2411-502: Let us define the set $I \subseteq~\Type(k+1, D, \Lambda)$, and for each $\psi \in I$ the label sets $\widehat{\Lambda ^\psi$, $\lca$-reduced formulas $\widehat{\psi}$ and $\hat{\Lambda ^\psi$-labeling $\widehat{L ^\psi$ of $F$ in the same way we defined them in \Cref{lem:elimination_forest .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-503: Furthermore, we define \begin{equation}\widehat{\Lambda} = \bigcup_{\psi \in I} \widehat{\Lambda}^\psi, \; \widehat{\varphi} = \bigvee_{\psi \in I} \widehat{\psi},  and, for every node $u\in F$,  \widehat{L}(u) = \bigcup_{\psi \in I} \widehat{L}^\psi(u).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-036: \begin{equation}\widehat{\Lambda} = \bigcup_{\psi \in I} \widehat{\Lambda}^\psi, \; \widehat{\varphi} = \bigvee_{\psi \in I} \widehat{\psi},  and, for every node $u\in F$,  \widehat{L}(u) = \bigcup_{\psi \in I} \widehat{L}^\psi(u).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-504: We then extend $\widehat{L}$ to the nodes of $G\smallsetminus F$, by merely defining $\widehat{L (v) = L(v)$ for all $v\notin V(F)$.}
elements.sent-2411-505: Since $\Type(k+1, d, \Lambda)$ is a finite set, it is enough to show that, for every $\psi\in I$, the label set $\widehat{\Lambda ^\psi$, and the formulas $\widehat{\psi}$ and $\widehat{L ^\psi$ can be computed in $\cO(D)$ rounds.}}
elements.sent-2411-506: In fact, the definitions of the set $I$, $\widehat{\Lambda}$ and $\widehat{\varphi}$ do not depend in the instance~$F$, so they can be computed at every node without any communication.
elements.sent-2411-507: It remains to compute, for any given $\psi\in I$, the value of $\widehat{L ^\psi(v)$ at each node~$v$.}
elements.sent-2411-509: Let $\gamma$ and $\delta$ be the functions such that $\psi = \type_{\gamma,\delta}$.
elements.sent-2411-510: Let $h_x$, $h_y$, and $h$ as defined in the proof of \Cref{lem:elimination_forest .}
elements.sent-2411-511: Observe that every node knows $\varphi$, and thus every node can compute $\psi$, $h_x$, $h_y$, and $h$ without any communication.
elements.sent-2411-512: Furthermore, thanks to the DFS traversal performed in each $T_i$, every node knows whether its depth is $h$, $h_x$, $h_y$ or none of them.
elements.sent-2411-513: We now revisit the three cases treated in the proof of \Cref{lem:elimination_forest .}
elements.sent-2411-514: In this case, the algorithm just needs to to identify the good nodes in $F$.
elements.sent-2411-515: To do so, for every $i\in $, every node $v$ at depth $h_y$ in $T_i$ checks whether it is a candidate node.
elements.sent-2411-516: Every such nodes does not need any communication for this, as it knows its depth in $T_i$, as well as $L(v)$ and $\gamma(v)$.
elements.sent-2411-517: Then, the algorithm broadcasts ping messages (on a single bit) downward every tree, from every candidate node.
elements.sent-2411-518: These ping messages are forwarded by their descendants downward every $T_i$, for $h_x-h$ rounds.
elements.sent-2411-519: More precisely, each candidate node sends a ping as a single bit~1 to all its children, and, for $h-h_x-1$ rounds, each node that receives that ping from its parent forwards it to all its children.
elements.sent-2411-520: Finally, every node $u$ at depth $h_x$ that receives the ping know that their ancestor at level $h_y$ are candidates, and thus they mark themselves as good nodes.
elements.sent-2411-521: Moreover, every such node $u$ sets $\widehat{L ^\psi(u) = L(u) \cup \{\good\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-522: Every other node $u$ in~$F$ sets $\widehat{L ^\psi(u) = L(u)$.}
elements.sent-2411-523: All these operations consume $h_x - h =\cO(d)$ rounds.
elements.sent-2411-524: In this case the algorithm is divided in two phases.
elements.sent-2411-525: Similar to previous case, the candidate nodes can identify themselves without any communication.
elements.sent-2411-526: In the first phase, ping messages are broadcasted upward in each tree~$T_i$, starting from the candidate nodes, during $h-h_y$ rounds.
elements.sent-2411-527: In other words, the candidate nodes send a single bit to their parents, and, for $h-h_y-1$ more rounds, each node that receives a ping in the previous round forwards it to its parent.
elements.sent-2411-528: During this phase, every node $u$ at depth $>h$ that receives a ping message is marked as a good node, and it adds $\good$ to $\widehat{L ^\psi(u)$.}
elements.sent-2411-530: During the second phase, each pivot node $z$ (i.e., every node at depth $h$) computes $\kappa(z)$ as the number of children that sent a ping message during the last round of the first phase.
elements.sent-2411-531: Then, each pivot node $z$ broadcast the value $\ell(\kappa(z))$ downward the trees, for $h-h_x$ rounds.
elements.sent-2411-532: After $h-h_x$ rounds, every node $u$ at depth $h_x$ has received the message $\ell(\kappa(z))$ from its pivot ancestor $z$, and it adds this label to $\widehat{L ^\psi(u)$.}

elements.sent-2411-560: Given a graph $G =(V,E)$, a decomposition forest $F$ of $G$ is a rooted spanning forest of $G$ satisfying that for every edge $e\in E$, one of its endpoints is an ancestor of the other in $F$.

elements.sent-2411-755: To prove this statement we will generalize the techniques of~\cite{FominFMRT24 , for proving \Cref{thm:FFMRT24 .}}
elements.sent-2411-756: We will only restate here the main features of their proof, based on tree decomposition and treewidth-related techniques.
elements.sent-2411-757: Recall that the tree decomposition of a graph $H=(V,E)$ is a pair $(T = (I,F), \{B_i\}_{i \in I )$ where $T$ is a tree and each $B_i$ is a subset of vertices of $V$, called bag of node $i$, such that.}
elements.sent-2411-758: The width of the decomposition is the maximum size of a bag, minus one.
elements.sent-2411-759: The treewidth of $H$ is the minimum width over all its tree decompositions.
elements.sent-2411-760: Graphs of treedepth at most $p$ have treewidth at most $2^p$~\cite{NesetrilOdM12 .}
elements.sent-2411-761: The proof of \Cref{thm:FFMRT24 is based on three ingredients.}
elements.sent-2411-762: The first ingredient is a dynamic programming sequential algorithm for decision, optimization and counting solutions of a given formula $\varphi(x) \in FO(\Lambda)$, over labeled graphs $(H,L)$ of bounded treewidth.
elements.sent-2411-763: Here we will assume that a tree decomposition of constant width is part of the input, and moreover the tree is rooted.
elements.sent-2411-764: The algorithm used in~\cite{FominFMRT24 is due to Borie, Parker and Tovey~\cite{BoPaTo92 .}}
elements.sent-2411-765: The nodes of the tree decomposition $(T = (I,F), \{B_i\}_{i \in I )$ are processed bottom-up, from the leaves to the root of $T$.}
elements.sent-2411-766: When processing node $i$, let $j_1,\dots,j_q$ be its children in $T$.
elements.sent-2411-767: The essential observation due to~\cite{BoPaTo92 is that the dynamic programming tables at node $i$ are of constant size for decision, and of $\cO(\log n)$ size for counting and optimization.}
elements.sent-2411-768: Moreover, for computing the information (encoding partial solutions) at node $i$, one only needs to know the subgraphs $H[B_i]$ and $H[B_{j_1 ], \dots, H[B_{j_q ]$ induced by the bags at node $i$ and its children, together with the information already computed at the children nodes.}}

elements.sent-2411-975: In this section we give evidence that our results somehow represent the limit of tractabilty of distributed model checking and distributed certification of properties on graphs of bounded expansion.
elements.sent-2411-976: All our reductions are based on classical results in communication complexity.
elements.sent-2411-977: Let $f: X \times Y \rightarrow \{0,1\}$ be a function.
elements.sent-2411-978: The deterministic communication complexity of $f$ $D(f)$ corresponds to the minimum number of bits that must be exchanged in any deterministic protocol between a player Alice holding an input $x \in X$ and another player Bob $y\in Y$ in order to output the value $f(x,y)$.
elements.sent-2411-979: The non-deterministic communication complexity of $f$, $N(f)$ is defined similarly, but for non-deterministic communication protocols.
elements.sent-2411-980: We refer to the book of Kushilevitz and Nissan for more detailed definitions \cite{kushilevitz1997communication .}
elements.sent-2411-981: In important problem in communication complexity is set disjointedness.
elements.sent-2411-983: For each $n$ the inputs of $\Disj_n$ are subsets of $[n]$.
elements.sent-2411-984: Given $A, B\subseteq [n]$ we have that $\Disj(A,B) = 1$ if and only if $A\cap B = \emptyset$.
elements.subsection-2411-013: No Extension to Monadic Second-Order Logic
elements.sent-2411-985: We first show that it is not possible to extend our results to the whole set of MSO properties on graphs of bounded expansion.
elements.sent-2411-986: We illustrate this fact by considering the property of being non-three-colorable.
elements.sent-2411-987: We define $\ntcol$ the set of graphs that do not admit a proper three-coloring (their chromatic number is at least $4$).
elements.sent-2411-988: The membership in $\ntcol$ can be represented by the following MSO formula: \begin{equation}\forall C_1, C_2, C_3, \exists x,y,~ \adj(x,y) \wedge \big((x\in C_1 \wedge y \in C_1)\vee (x\in C_2 \wedge y \in C_2) \vee (x\in C_3 \wedge y \in C_3)\big)\end{equation} In the following theorem, we show that there are no efficient \CONGEST\ algorithms for checking nor compact certification scheme for certifying the membership in $\ntcol$, even under the promise that the input graph belongs to a class of graphs of bounded expansion and of logarithmic diameter.
elements.formula-2411-074: \begin{equation}\forall C_1, C_2, C_3, \exists x,y,~ \adj(x,y) \wedge \big((x\in C_1 \wedge y \in C_1)\vee (x\in C_2 \wedge y \in C_2) \vee (x\in C_3 \wedge y \in C_3)\big)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-989: Let $\mathcal{G}$ the class of graphs of maximum degree $5$ and diameter $\cO(\log n)$.
elements.sent-2411-990: Every \CONGEST\ algorithm deciding the membership in $\ntcol$ of a $n$-node input graph in $\mathcal{G}$ requires $\Omega(n/\log^2 n)$ rounds of communication.
elements.sent-2411-991: Every certification scheme that certifies membership to $\ntcol$ of a $n$-node input graph in $\mathcal{G}$ requires certificates of size $\Omega(n/\log n)$.
elements.sent-2411-992: The same proof directly implies the same lowerbound for the number of rounds of a \CONGEST\ algorithm deciding the membership in $\ntcol$.
elements.sent-2411-993: We give an sketch of the proof of Göös and Suomella and then we show how to adapt it to the case when the input graph has maximum degree $4$.
elements.sent-2411-994: The proof of \cite{GoosS16 is consists in a reduction to $\Disj_{n^2}$.}
elements.sent-2411-995: In the reduction each instance $(A,B)$ of $\Disj_{n^2}$ is associated with a $O(n)$-node graph $G_{A,B}$.
elements.sent-2411-996: The set of vertices of $G_{A,B}$ is divided in two sets of vertices, denoted $V_A$ and $V_B$, and satisfies the following conditions:.
elements.sent-2411-997: The graph induced by $V_A$ only depends on $A$.
elements.sent-2411-998: The graph induced by $V_B$ only depends on $B$.
elements.sent-2411-999: The set of edges $E_{A,B}$ with one endpoint in $V_A$ and the other in $V_B$ contains at most $\cO(\log n)$ edges, and does not depend on $A$ and $B$, only on $n$.
elements.sent-2411-1000: The nodes with endpoints in $E_{A,B}$ are denoted $V_{A,B}$.
elements.sent-2411-1001: Then, given a certification scheme $\mathcal{P}$ for $\ntcol$ with certificates of size $c(n)$ on $n$-node graphs, we build the following non-deterministic protocol for $\Disj_{n^2}$.
elements.sent-2411-1002: First, Alice and Bob build graph $G_{A,B}$, where Alice builds the subgraph depending on $A$, i.e., $V_A$ and all edges incident to $V_A$, and Bob the part depending on $B$.
elements.sent-2411-1003: Then, non-deterministically each player generate the certificates of all the nodes of $G_{A,B}$, and communicate to the other player the certificates generated for the nodes in the cut.
elements.sent-2411-1004: Then, the players run the verification algorithm of $\mathcal{P}$ and accept if all the nodes on their side accept.
elements.sent-2411-1005: We have that both players accept if and only if $G_{A,B}\in \ntcol$, which holds if and only if $A\cap B = \emptyset$.
elements.sent-2411-1006: The total communication is $\cO(\log n \cdot c(n))$.
elements.sent-2411-1007: Using \Cref{prop:lowerboundDISJ we obtain that $\cO(\log n \cdot c(n)) = N(\Disj_{n^2 ) = \Omega(n^2)$.}}

elements.sent-2411-1018: Given an $n$-node graph $G$, the reduction picks an arbitrary graph $G$ and constructs a graph $\widetilde{G}$ of maximum degree $4$, such that $G\in \ntcol$ if and only if $\widetilde{G \in \ntcol$.}
elements.sent-2411-1019: Roughly, the we can construct $\widetilde{G}$ replacing each node $v$ of degree greater than $4$ by a node gadget, as the one represented in \Cref{fig:Thm5-2 .}
elements.sent-2411-1020: Let us denote $d_v$ the degree of $v$.
elements.sent-2411-1021: The gadget consists in a set $S_v$ of $7(d_v-2) + 1$ nodes of degree at most $4$.
elements.sent-2411-1022: In $S_v$ there are $d_v$ nodes $v_1, \dots, v_{d_v}$ satisfying that on every proper three-coloring of $\widetilde{G}$, these nodes must be colored the same.
elements.sent-2411-1023: Furthermore, every coloring that assigns $v_1, \dots, v_{d_v}$ the same color can be extended to a proper $3$-coloring of the whole gadget.

elements.sent-2411-1026: Then, in $\widetilde{G}$ we add the edge $\{u_i, v_j\}$.
