elements.sent-2411-1103: In this paper, we have established that, for every FO formula~$\varphi$, and every graph class $\mathcal{G}$ of bounded expansion, there is a distributed model checking algorithm deciding whether $G\models \varphi$ in $\cO(D+\log n)$ rounds in the \CONGEST model, for every graph $G\in \mathcal{G}$.
elements.sent-2411-1104: In the distributed framework, this result can be viewed as an analog of the \FPT algorithms for model-checking FO formulas on classes of bounded expansion derived in~\cite{DvorakKT13 for centralized computing.}
elements.sent-2411-1105: A natural question is whether our result could be extended to a more general class of sparse graphs.
elements.sent-2411-1106: In the centralized model checking, the fixed-parameter tractability of FO was extended from graphs of bounded expansion to a more general, namely nowhere dense, class of graphs in~\cite{GroheKS14 .}
elements.sent-2411-1107: Note that nowhere dense graphs represent the ultimate limit of the program in designing fixed-parameter tractable algorithms for model checking FO formulas.
elements.sent-2411-1108: Indeed, as proven in~\cite{DvorakKT13 , for any graph class $\mathcal{G}$ that is not nowhere dense (i.e., $\mathcal{G}$ is {\em{somewhere dense ), and is closed under taking subgraphs, model checking FO formulas on $\mathcal{G}$ is not fixed-parameter tractable (unless $\FPT=\W{1}$).}}}
elements.sent-2411-1109: Where the tractability border for \CONGEST is unknown.
elements.sent-2411-1110: The question is therefore twofold: (1)~Is it possible to design model checking algorithms for FO formulas running in $\tilde{O (D)$ rounds in \CONGEST for nowhere dense graphs of diameter~$D$? (2)~Is the class of nowhere dense graphs the ultimate limit of this program under the \CONGEST model? There are several difficulties in extending our approach to nowhere dense graphs.}
elements.sent-2411-1111: First, it is unclear how to implement the combinatorial arguments from \cite{GroheKS14 , particularly the constructions of sparse neighborhood covers, in distributed model.}
elements.sent-2411-1112: Second, the proof of \cite{GroheKS14 builds on a weak variant of Gaifman local form and not a quantifier elimination procedure.}
elements.sent-2411-1113: While this is sufficient for fixed-parameter tractability of FO model-checking, we do not know whether it could be adapted in the distributed setting.
elements.sent-2411-1114: As far as our specific results are concerned, two questions arise.
elements.sent-2411-1115: First, could we remove the additional logarithmic term from the complexity of our model checking algorithm? This term reflects the coloring algorithm's complexity in~\cite{NesetrilM16 for graph classes of bounded expansion.}
elements.sent-2411-1116: So, the question is whether it is possible to compute such a coloring in $\cO(D)$ rounds, or even in a constant number of rounds.
elements.sent-2411-1117: Another intriguing question is already asked in~\cite{NesetrilM16 , which we report here.}
elements.sent-2411-1118: The question is whether it is possible, for every graph class of bounded expansion, to design an $\cO(\log n)$-round algorithm in \CONGEST for identifying all nodes satisfying a ``local'' FO formula~$\varphi$, i.e., to mark all nodes $v$ such that $\varphi(v)$ holds, where $\varphi(x)$ refers only to nodes at constant distance from~$x$.. \bibliographystyle{siam.}
elements.sent-2411-1119: The sparsity theory of Ne\v{s et\v{r il and Ossona de Mendez provides generic combinatorial and algorithmic methods suitable for many classes of sparse graphs.}}
elements.sent-2411-1120: As such, it has already driven major advances in model checking, parameterized complexity, and approximation algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-1121: We show that this theory is equally well suited to the design of distributed algorithms, providing the first generic algorithmic results for distributed first-order (FO) model checking on graphs of bounded expansion.
elements.sent-2411-1122: The latter form a rich graph family which subsumes planar, bounded-genus, bounded-treedepth, bounded-treewidth, and bounded-degree graphs, as well as graphs excluding a fixed minor or topological minor, sparse Erdős–Rényi graphs (a.a.s.), and several network models such as stochastic block models for suitable parameter ranges.
elements.sent-2411-1123: Our main results are the following algorithmic meta-theorems for distributed algorithms in the standard \CONGEST model on every graph class $\mathcal{G}$ of bounded expansion.
elements.sent-2411-1124: First, we resolve an open problem originally posed by Ne\v{s et\v{r il and Ossona de Mendez (Distributed Computing~2016), and reiterated by Pilipczuk, Siebertz, and Toruńczyk (LICS~2018).}}
elements.sent-2411-1125: Recall that a formula $\varphi(x)$ is local if the satisfaction of $\varphi(x)$ depends only on the $r$-neighbourhood of its free variable~$x$, for some fixed $r$.
elements.sent-2411-1126: For instance, the formula ``$x$ belongs to a triangle'' is local.
elements.sent-2411-1127: We show that, for every local FO formula $\varphi(x)$, there exists a deterministic algorithm that, for every $n$-vertex graph $G\in \mathcal{G}$, identifies all vertices $v\in V(G)$ such that $G\models \varphi(v)$, in $\cO(\log n)$ rounds.
elements.sent-2411-1128: Second, we show that, for every FO formula~$\varphi$, there is a deterministic algorithm that, for every $n$-vertex graph $G\in \mathcal{G}$, decides whether $G\models \varphi$, in $\mathcal{O (D+\log n)$ rounds, where $D$ is the diameter of $G$.}
elements.sent-2411-1129: Note that the dependence on~$D$ is unavoidable, as even the simple FO formula ``there exist two vertices of degree~3'' needs $\Omega(D)$ rounds in \CONGEST, even on trees.
elements.sent-2411-1130: The techniques behind these meta-theorems extend to distributed counting, optimization, and certification problems on graphs of bounded expansion.
elements.sent-2411-1131: What is a sparse graph? The answer to this question depends on the perspective.
elements.sent-2411-1132: For instance, the graph class in which every graph is obtained from a complete graph by subdividing every edge once is ``combinatorially sparse'' (it has a number of edges linear in the number of vertices), but is not necessarily ``effectively sparse'' from an algorithmic perspective.
elements.sent-2411-1133: The sparsity theory by Ne\v{s et\v{r il and Ossona de Mendez \cite{NesetrilM06,NesetrilOdM12 provided us with an approach of sparsity that can be effectively used algorithmically.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1134: Within the sparsity theory, a crucial role is assigned to the graph classes of bounded expansion.
elements.sent-2411-1135: Intuitively, a graph class~$\mathcal{G}$ has bounded expansion if there exists a function $f:\mathbb{N}\to \mathbb{N}$ such that, for every $G\in \mathcal{G}$, and any $r\geq 0$, every minor~$H$ of~$G$ obtained by contracting disjoint subgraphs of radius at most~$r$ is $f(r)$-degenerate (i.e., every subgraph of $H$ has a vertex of degree at most~$f(r)$).
elements.sent-2411-1136: For instance, the aforementioned graph class containing every graph obtained from a complete graph by subdividing every edge once is not of bounded expansion.
elements.sent-2411-1137: Graph classes with bounded expansion form a rich graph family (see Figure~\ref{fig:graphclasse ).}
elements.sent-2411-1138: For instance, the class of planar graphs, the class of graphs with bounded genus, the class of graphs of bounded degree, and the class of minor-free graphs and topologically minor-free graphs are all of bounded expansion, as well as sparse Erdős–Rényi graphs (a.a.s.).
elements.sent-2411-1139: Moreover, \cite{DemaineRRVSS19 demonstrated that many network models (e.g., stochastic block models) exhibit bounded expansion for some ranges of parameters, and these findings are even supported with empirical measurements on a corpus of real-world networks.}
elements.sent-2411-1140: In particular, it has already driven major advances in model checking~\cite{DvorakKT13 , parameterized complexity~\cite{NesetrilM06 , kernelization \cite{drange2016kernelization,eickmeyer2017neighborhood and approximation algorithms~\cite{Har-PeledQ17 .}}}}
elements.subsubsection-2411-016: Distributed Computing in Graphs of Bounded Expansion
elements.sent-2411-1141: Ne\v{s et\v{r il and Ossona de Mendez also showed that bounded expansion can also be approached from a distributed computing perspective (see \cite{NesetrilM16 ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-1142: Their finding is based on the following characterization~\cite{NesetrilOdM12 .}
elements.sent-2411-1143: A class $\mathcal{G}$ of graphs has bounded expansion if and only if there exists a function $g:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ such that, for every $G\in\mathcal{G}$, and for every $k\in \mathbb{N}$, there exists a coloring of the vertices of $G$ with colors in $[g(k)]=\{1,\dots,g(k)\}$ such that, for every set $U\subseteq [g(k)]$ of cardinality at most~$k$, the subgraph of~$G$ induced by the nodes colored with the colors in~$U$ has treedepth at most~$|U|$.
elements.sent-2411-1144: The function $g$ depends on the given bound $f$ on the expansion of~$\mathcal{G}$.
elements.subsubsection-2411-017: Deciding Local First-Order Formulas in CONGEST
elements.sent-2411-1145: The fact that a coloring satisfying the requirement of bounded expansion can be computed in a distributed manner yields the question of whether this ability can be used as a building block for solving ``hard problems'' efficiently in \CONGEST, in graph classes of bounded expansion.
elements.sent-2411-1146: In the context of \CONGEST, ``hard problems'' are usually thought as problems requiring at least $poly (n)$ rounds to be solved, whereas ``easy problems'' are problems solvable in $poly\log(n)$ rounds.
elements.sent-2411-1147: As a concrete illustration, it is known~\cite{DruckerKO13 that deciding $C_4$-freeness is hard in \CONGEST, as it requires $\tilde{\Omega (\sqrt{n )$ rounds --- in the distributed setting, deciding a property asks all nodes to accept if the property is satisfied, and at least one node to reject if the property is violated.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1148: Is it possible to overcome the $\tilde{\Omega (\sqrt{n )$ lower bound for deciding $C_4$-freeness in graphs of bounded expansion? Ne\v{s et\v{r il and Ossona de Mendez~\cite{NesetrilM16 actually answered positively to this question, by highlighting a general approach for deciding $H$-freeness, in $\mathcal{O (\log n)$ rounds, for any fixed (connected) graph~$H$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1149: In fact, this approach can even be used to mark all nodes $v$ such that $v$ belongs to some subgraph of $G$ isomorphic to~$H$.
elements.sent-2411-1150: However, they asked a broader and more challenging question, reiterated by Pilipczuk, Siebertz, and Toruńczyk \cite{PilipczukST18 .}
elements.sent-2411-1151: Recall that a formula $\varphi(x)$ is local if the satisfaction of $\varphi(x)$ depends only on the $r$-neighborhood of its free variable~$x$, for some fixed~$r$.
elements.sent-2411-1152: For instance, in graphs, the formula ``node $x$ belongs to a 4-cycle'' is indeed local.
elements.sent-2411-1153: The question in~\cite{NesetrilM16,PilipczukST18 goes way beyond deciding $H$-freeness.}
elements.sent-2411-1154: It can be formulated as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-1155: Our first main result is a positive answer to the open problem in~\cite{NesetrilM16,PilipczukST18 .}
elements.sent-2411-1156: That is, we show that, for every local FO formula $\varphi(x)$, and for every graph class $\mathcal{G}$ of bounded expansion, there exists a deterministic algorithm that, for every $n$-vertex graph $G\in \mathcal{G}$, marks all vertices $v\in V(G)$ such that $G\models \varphi(v)$, in $\cO(\log n)$ rounds in the \CONGEST\ model.
elements.sent-2411-1157: In fact, this result also holds for FO formulas on labeled graphs, i.e., graphs in which every node is provided with labels drawn from a finite set~$\Lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-1158: That is, the formulas may also include unary predicates on node labels.
elements.sent-2411-1159: Let us call a $\Lambda$-labeled graph any graph whose nodes are label by labels in~$\Lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-1160: A $\Lambda$-labeled graph is thus a pair $(G,\ell)$ where $G=(V,E)$ is a graph, and $\ell:V\to 2^\Lambda$ is a label-assignment to the nodes of~$G$.
elements.sent-2411-1161: Our first main theorem is the following: \begin{restatable {theorem {thmFOlocal Let $\Lambda$ be a finite set.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1162: For every local FO formula $\varphi(x)$ on $\Lambda$-labeled graphs, and for every class of graphs $\mathcal{G}$ of bounded expansion, there exists a deterministic algorithm that, for every $n$-node network $G=(V,E)\in \mathcal{G}$, and every $\ell:V\to 2^\Lambda$, marks all vertices $v\in V$ such that $(G,\ell)\models\varphi(v)$, in $\cO(\log n)$ rounds under the \CONGEST model.
elements.sent-2411-1163: An immediate corollary of our result on local FO formulas is that, for every formula $\varphi$ such as $\varphi=\forall x\; \psi(x)$ where $\psi(x)$ is a local FO formula (e.g., $H$-freeness), and for every graph class $\mathcal{G}$ of bounded expansion, deciding whether $G\models \varphi$ can be done in $\cO(\log n)$ rounds in the \CONGEST\ model (by merely applying our algorithm to $\neg\psi(x)$, and letting every marked node reject).
elements.sent-2411-1164: However, Gaifman’s theorem~\cite{gaifman1982local tells us that not all FO formulas are as simple as that.}
elements.sent-2411-1165: For instance, even the simple FO formula ``there exist two vertices of degree~3'' needs $\Omega(D)$ rounds to be checked in \CONGEST, where $D$ denotes the diameter of the graph --- this lower bound holds even for trees.
elements.sent-2411-1166: In other words, a dependence on~$D$ is unavoidable, even for trees.
elements.sent-2411-1167: Nevertheless, our second main result states that, for every FO formula~$\varphi$, and for every graph class $\mathcal{G}$ of bounded expansion, there exists a deterministic algorithm that, for every $n$-vertex graph $G\in \mathcal{G}$ of diameter~$D$, decides whether $G\models \varphi$, in $\mathcal{O (D+\log n)$ rounds.}
elements.sent-2411-1168: Again, our result holds for labeled graphs too, and is formally stated as follows: \begin{restatable {theorem {thmFOexpCONG Let $\Lambda$ be a finite set.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1169: For every FO formula $\varphi$ on $\Lambda$-labeled graphs, and for every class of graphs $\mathcal{G}$ of bounded expansion, there exists a distributed algorithm that, for every $n$-node network $G=(V,E)\in \mathcal{G}$ of diameter~$D$, and every $\ell:V\to 2^\Lambda$, decides whether $(G,\ell)\models\varphi$ in $\cO(D+\log n)$ rounds under the \CONGEST model.
elements.sent-2411-1170: Indeed, we show that the mere FO formula $C_6$-freeness requires $\tilde{\Omega (\sqrt{n )$ rounds to be checked in \CONGEST, even in graphs of degeneracy~2 and of diameter~6 (see \Cref{thm:lowerboundC6degeneracy ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-1171: Moreover, our result is the best that one can hope regarding the class of formulas, in the sense that it cannot be extended to, e.g., MSO.
elements.sent-2411-1172: Our results extend beyond model checking, in several directions.
elements.sent-2411-1173: First, we consider the problem of counting solutions of FO formulas with free variables.
elements.sent-2411-1174: We actually consider two forms of counting:.
elements.sent-2411-1175: Local counting: Given a local formula $\varphi(x_1,\dots,x_k)$ with $k\geq 1$ free variables, such as $\adj(x_1,x_2)\land \adj(x_2,x_3) \land \adj(x_3,x_1)$ (i.e., $x_1,x_2,x_3$ form a triangle), each node $v$ of the considered graph $G=(V,E)$ outputs a (possibly negative) integer $\nu(v)$ satisfying that the number of tuples $v_1,\dots,v_k$ for which $(G,\ell)\models \varphi(v_1,\dots,v_k)$ equals $\sum_{v\in V}\nu(v)$.
elements.sent-2411-1176: Global counting: Given a general formula $\varphi(x_1,\dots,x_k)$ with $k\geq 1$ free variables, all nodes output a same value equal to the number of tuples $v_1,\dots,v_k$ for which $(G,\ell)\models \varphi(v_1,\dots,v_k)$.
elements.sent-2411-1177: e.g., counting the $k$-tuples of nodes of degree~3.
elements.sent-2411-1178: For every FO formula $\varphi(x_1,\dots,x_k)$ on $\Lambda$-labeled graphs with $k\geq 1$ free variables $x_1,\dots,x_k$, and for every class of graphs $\mathcal{G}$ of bounded expansion, there exists a distributed algorithm that, for every $n$-node network $G=(V,E)\in \mathcal{G}$ of diameter~$D$ and every $\ell:V\to 2^\Lambda$, performs global counting in ${O(D+\log n)}$ rounds in \CONGEST.
elements.sent-2411-1179: If the formula $\varphi(x_1,\dots,x_k)$ is local then there exists a distributed algorithm that performs local counting in $\cO(\log n)$ rounds in \CONGEST.
elements.sent-2411-1180: Some optimization problems can also be expressed as finding optimal solutions for FO formulas with free variables, over weighted graphs, e.g., finding a maximum-weight triangle.
elements.sent-2411-1181: For every FO formula $\varphi(x_1,\dots,x_k)$ on $\Lambda$-labeled graphs with $k\geq 1$ free variables $x_1,\dots,x_k$, and for every class graphs $\mathcal{G}$ of bounded expansion, there exists a distributed algorithm that, for every $n$-node network $G=(V,E)\in \mathcal{G}$ of diameter~$D$, every $\ell:V\to 2^\Lambda$, and every weight function $\omega:V\to \mathbb{N}$, computes a $k$-tuple of vertices $(v_1,\dots,v_k)$ such that $(G,\ell)\models\varphi(v_1,\dots,v_k)$ and $\sum_{i=1 ^k\omega(v_i)$ is maximum, in $\cO(D+\log n)$ rounds under the \CONGEST model.}
elements.sent-2411-1182: Roughly, a distributed certification scheme~\cite{GoosS16,KormanKP10 for a graph property~$\varphi$ is a pair prover-verifier.}
elements.sent-2411-1183: The prover is a centralized, computationally unlimited oracle that assigns certificates to the nodes.
elements.sent-2411-1184: The verifier is a 1-round distributed algorithm whose role is to check whether the certificates assigned to the nodes form a proof that the graph satisfies the property.
elements.sent-2411-1185: A distributed certification scheme is correct if it satisfies completeness (i.e., if $(G,\ell)\models \varphi$, then the prover can assign certificates such that the verifier accepts at all nodes), and soundness (i.e., if $(G,\ell)\not\models \varphi$, then, no matter the certificates assigned to the nodes, the verifier rejects in at least one node).
elements.sent-2411-1186: The main complexity measure is the size of the certificates assigned by the prover on legal instances.
elements.sent-2411-1187: For every FO formula $\varphi$ on $\Lambda$-labeled graphs, and for every class of graphs $\mathcal{G}$ of bounded expansion, there exists a distributed certification scheme that, for every $n$-node network $G=(V,E) \in \mathcal{G}$ and every $\ell: V\rightarrow 2^{\Lambda}$, certifies $(G,\ell) \models \varphi$ using certificates on $\cO(\log n)$ bits.
elements.sent-2411-1188: Note also that our certification scheme is actually a proof-labeling scheme~\cite{KormanKP10 , which is a more demanding form of distributed certification than, e.g., locally checkable proofs~\cite{GoosS16 .}}
elements.sent-2411-1189: More generally, \cite{FeuilloleyBP22 shows that every MSO formula can be certified with $O(\log n)$-bit certificates in graphs of bounded treedepth.}
elements.sent-2411-1190: Indeed, we show that it does not extend to MSO, as certifying non-3-colorability requires certificates of $\tilde{\Omega (\sqrt{n )$ bits in planar graphs (see \Cref{thm:lowerboundMSOinPlanar ), and even certificates of $\tilde{\Theta (n)$ bits in bounded degree graphs (see \Cref{thm:lowerboundMSOinEXP ).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1191: The open problem by Ne\v{s et\v{r il and Ossona de Mendez was actually stated for the \BCONGEST model, i.e., a weaker version of the \CONGEST\/ model in which, at every round, each node $v$ must send the same $\cO(\log n)$-bit message to all its neighbors (see, e.g., ~\cite{DruckerKO13,korhonen_et_al:LIPIcs:2018:8625 ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-1192: However, (1)~the problem is not necessarily easier to solve in the general version of the \CONGEST model, and (2)~we solve it anyway by the affirmative using an algorithm designed for the \BCONGEST model, and the complexity of our algorithm is expressed under the latter.
elements.sent-2411-1193: For the sake of simplifying the notations, we simply refer to \CONGEST in the statements of our theorems, but all our upper bound results (but \Cref{thm:thmFOexpOPT ) hold under the \BCONGEST model too\footnote{The main reason why \Cref{thm:thmFOexpOPT does not hold in \BCONGEST is that our algorithm involves an information dissemination phase going downward a tree, in which different messages are sent to different children.. .}}}
elements.sent-2411-1194: In a nutshell, our technical contribution is twofold:.
elements.sent-2411-1195: The first contribution is an adaptation of the quantifier elimination method \cite{DvorakKT13,GroheK09,PilipczukST18 from centralized computing to the distributed setting.}
elements.sent-2411-1196: On a general level, we primarily follow the steps of the centralized approach.
elements.sent-2411-1197: Yet, this adaptation is highly non-trivial, as we had to perform all operations locally without incurring a high volume of communication between adjacent nodes.
elements.sent-2411-1198: The second, and perhaps main, contribution of the paper is the novel locality preserving quantifier elimination method.
elements.sent-2411-1199: Specifically, given a local FO formula $\varphi(x)$, we show how to eliminate its quantifiers, i.e., producing a quantifier-free formula $\widehat{\varphi (x)$ that is equivalent to $\varphi(x)$, without losing locality.}
elements.sent-2411-1200: This is achieved by generalizing the notion of locality to formulas with more than a single free variable in a specific way, thereby enabling the application of induction on the number of quantifiers while preserving locality.
elements.sent-2411-1201: More specifically, our approach to establish Theorem~\ref{thm:thmFOlocal is mainly based on the quantifier elimination methods from \cite{PilipczukST18 .}}
elements.sent-2411-1202: Recall that, given an FO formula $\varphi$, and a class $\mathcal{G}$ of graphs with expansion~$f$, quantifier elimination transforms every FO formula $\varphi$ into a quantifier-free FO formula $\widehat\varphi$ such that, for every $G=(V,E)\in \mathcal{G}$, and every labeling $\ell:V\to 2^\Lambda$, one can construct a node-labeling $\widehat{\ell}$ of $G$ such that \begin{equation}(G,\ell)\models\varphi\iff (G,\widehat{\ell})\models\widehat\varphi.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1203: In full generality, quantifier elimination allows the graph $G$ to be transformed into a graph~$\widehat G$, with $(G,\ell)\models\varphi\iff (\widehat{G ,\widehat{\ell )\models\widehat\varphi$.}}
elements.sent-2411-1204: However, as we shall discuss later, the approach in~\cite{PilipczukST18 preserves the graph~$G$.}
elements.sent-2411-1205: Yet, we have to overcome two main difficulties for computing~$\widehat\ell$.
elements.sent-2411-1206: First, we have to implement quantifier elimination in the distributed setting.
elements.sent-2411-1207: That is, we show how to compute the new labeling $\widehat\ell$ of~$G$, and the new formula~$\widehat\varphi$ efficiently in the \CONGEST model.
elements.sent-2411-1208: Note that for an algorithm to be efficient in \CONGEST, not only should the exchange of information be restricted to nearby nodes in the graph, but also the amount of information exchanged between neighbors should be small.
elements.sent-2411-1209: For instance, in $\cO(\log n)$ rounds, a node with degree $\gg\log n$ cannot directly send the list of all its neighbors' identifiers to any of its neighbors.
elements.sent-2411-1210: Second, we have to adapt quantifier elimination for preserving locality.
elements.sent-2411-1211: Given $G,\ell$, and a local FO formula $\varphi$, we show how to compute $\widehat\ell$ and $\widehat\varphi$ so that not only $\widehat\varphi$ is quantifier-free, and satisfies, for every $v\in V$, \begin{equation}(G,\ell)\models\varphi(v)\iff (G,\widehat{\ell})\models\widehat\varphi(v),\end{equation} but also every stage of the quantifier elimination process preserves locality.
elements.sent-2411-1212: This is achieved, in particular, by extending the natural ``semantic'' notion of local formula $\varphi(x)$ with a unique free variable, to a ``syntactic'' notion of local formula $\psi(x,\overline y)$ involving more than one free variables, i.e., $\bar{y =(y_1,\dots,y_k)$ is a vector of free variables.}
elements.sent-2411-1213: This syntactical notion is based on relativization, which is, in our framework, defining a new structure by restricting its domain to elements satisfying a form of mutual proximity.
elements.sent-2411-1214: Let us now enter a bit more into the details.
elements.sent-2411-1215: In order to build the pair $(\widehat{\varphi ,\widehat{\ell )$ from $(\varphi,G,\ell)$, the main issue is the elimination of the existential quantifiers (universal quantifiers can be handled as negated existential quantifiers).}}
elements.sent-2411-1216: The basic step is therefore the elimination of one existential quantifier.
elements.sent-2411-1217: Let us consider a formula $\psi(x,\bar{y )=\exists z \, \psi'(x,\bar{y ,z)$ where $\bar{y =(y_1,\dots,y_k)$ is a vector of free variables --- these variables are actually quantified in the original formula $\varphi(x)$, but the corresponding quantifiers will be eliminated at further steps.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1218: Let us assume that a pair $(\widehat{\psi' ,\ell')$ has already been computed from $(\psi',G,\ell)$, i.e., $\widehat{\psi'}$ is quantifier-free, and $\ell'$ is the appropriate new node-labeling of~$G$.}
elements.sent-2411-1219: The goal is then to compute $(\widehat{\psi ,\widehat{\ell )$ from $(\widehat{\psi' ,G,\ell')$ where $\widehat{\psi}$ is quantifier-free, and $\widehat{\ell}$ is the appropriate new node-labeling.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1220: The existential quantifier in $\psi(x,\bar{y )=\exists z \, \widehat{\psi' (x,\bar{y ,z)$ is eliminated in two stages.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1221: First, recall that the aforementioned characterization theorem from~\cite{NesetrilM06 states that, for any graph class $\mathcal{G}$ of expansion~$f$, there exists a function $g:\mathbb{N}\to \mathbb{N}$ satisfying that, for every $k\geq 1$, the vertices of any graph $G\in \mathcal{G}$ can be colored using a palette of $g(k)$ colors such that, for any set $U$ of at most $k$ colors, the subgraph of $G$ induced by the vertices with colors in~$U$ has treedepth at most~$k$.}
elements.sent-2411-1222: By setting $k=|\psi|$, i.e., by letting $k$ be the number of variables occurring in~$\psi$, it is guaranteed that at least one set $U$ of $k$ colors captures $k$ nodes for which $\psi$ holds whenever such nodes exist.
elements.sent-2411-1223: For instance, for $C_4$ freeness, for any coloring of the nodes with at least 4 colors, there exists a set $U$ of at most 4 colors such that, whenever there is a 4-cycle in the graph, this 4-cycle appears in the subgraph induced by the vertices colored with some color in~$U$.
elements.sent-2411-1224: Assuming given an appropriate $g(k)$-coloring, the quantifier elimination can proceed by considering all sets $U$ of $k$ colors, and eliminating the existential quantifier in graphs of bounded treedepth.
elements.sent-2411-1225: The second stage involves eventually eliminating the existential quantifier by transforming a formula on labeled graphs of bounded treedepth with an existential quantifier into a formula on labeled forests of bounded depth without existential quantifiers.
elements.sent-2411-1226: To encode the subgraph pattern, new unary and binary predicates are introduced.
elements.sent-2411-1227: These unary predicates are called label predicates.
elements.sent-2411-1228: Their aim is to specify the presence of certain symbols in the labels assigned to the nodes.
elements.sent-2411-1229: The new binary predicates are called least common ancestor predicates, or simply $\lca$ predicate for short.
elements.sent-2411-1230: Their aim is to specify the depth of the least common ancestor of two nodes in certain trees of the forest.
elements.sent-2411-1231: The subgraph patterns are encoded as Boolean combinations of label and $\lca$ predicates.
elements.sent-2411-1232: A first novelty of this paper is to adapt the approach of \cite{PilipczukST18 such that each step preserves the locality property of the considered formula.}
elements.sent-2411-1233: To that end, we extend the notion of local formula to more that one free variables: Roughly, a formula $\psi(x,\bar y)$ is local if (1)~$(G,\ell)\models \varphi(x,\bar y)$ implies that $\bar{y}\subseteq B_G(x,r)$, i.e., each $y_i$ is at distance at most $r$ from~$x$, and (2)~for every two graphs $G$ and $G'$, and every two vertices $x$ and $x'$ of $G$ and~$G'$, respectively, if there is a (label preserving) isomorphism $h:B_G(x,r)\to B_{G' (x',r)$ satisfying $h(x)=x'$, then $(G,\ell)\models \varphi(x,\bar{y )\iff (G',\ell)\models \varphi(h(x),h(\bar{y ))$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1234: This ``semantic'' definition is difficult to use in practice, which is why we introduce a ``syntactic'' notion of local form for local formulas.
elements.sent-2411-1235: For this purpose, we introduce new predicates, called distance predicates.
elements.sent-2411-1236: These predicates are of the form $\ball(y,x,r)$, interpreted as ``$y$ is in the ball of radius~$r$ around $x$ in the graph''.
elements.sent-2411-1237: The distance predicates allow us to syntactically enforce the locality property of a formula.
elements.sent-2411-1238: For instance, in a formula \(\ball(y,x,r) \wedge \varphi(x,y)\), the distance predicate enforces the fact that \(x\) and \(y\) are at bounded distance.
elements.sent-2411-1239: At each step of the quantifier elimination process, we make sure that both the original formula \(\varphi\), and the transformed formula \(\widehat\varphi\) explicitly contain distance predicates that guarantee their locality.
elements.sent-2411-1240: The presence of negations in the formula, potentially induced by universal quantifiers treated as negated existential quantifiers, must be treated with care.
elements.sent-2411-1241: Indeed, the locality property is not stable by negation.
elements.sent-2411-1242:
For instance, the negation of \( all(y,x,r) \wedge \varphi(x,y)\) is \(
eg all(y,x,r) \vee 
eg\varphi(x,y)\), which is not local because $
eg all(y,x,r)$ is satisfied for nodes $y$ that are far away from~$x$.
elements.sent-2411-1243: To overcome this issue, we introduce redundancy in the syntax of local formulas.
elements.sent-2411-1244: That is, instead of adding distance predicates once in the formula (for instance, immediately after the innermost quantifier), we duplicate the distance predicates wherever they are needed.
elements.sent-2411-1245: For instance, let us consider a formula $\varphi(x,\bar y)=\forall z\, \psi(x,\bar y,z)$ that is assumed to be local.
elements.sent-2411-1246: As said before, quantifier elimination proceeds by eliminating existential quantifiers, and thus this formula is rewritten as $\varphi(x,\bar y)=\neg\exists z\, \neg \psi(x,\bar y,z)$.
elements.sent-2411-1247: Now, we make the locality of $z$ explicit by rewriting $\varphi$ as \begin{equation}\varphi(x,\bar y)= \neg\exists z\, \big(\ball(z,x,r)\land \neg \psi(x,\bar y,z)\big).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1248: Moreover, we use redundancy, and reinforce the locality of $\bar y$ by introducing another distance predicate: \begin{equation}\varphi(x,\bar y)=\ball(\bar y,x,r)\land \neg\exists z\, \big(\ball(z,x,r)\land \neg \psi(x,\bar y,z)\big).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1249:
By distributing the distance predicate on~$ ar y$, we get  egin{equation}\varphi(x, ar y)= all( ar y,x,r)\land 
eg\exists z\,  ig( all( ar y,x,r)\land  all(z,x,r)\land 
eg \psi(x, ar y,z) ig),\end{equation} which can be rewritten as  egin{equation}\varphi(x, ar y)= all( ar y,x,r)\land 
eg\exists z\,  ig( all(( ar y,z),x,r)\land 
eg \psi(x, ar y,z) ig).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1250: As a result, the potential loss of locality resulting from the negation of $\psi(x,\bar y,z)$ is recovered thanks to the (redundant) distance predicate, as $\ball((\bar y,z),x,r)\land \neg \psi(x,\bar y,z)$ is local.
elements.sent-2411-1251: Eliminating the existential quantifier in this formula yields a (local) quantifier-free formula.
elements.sent-2411-1252: Negation is, however, applied a second time, which may result in losing locality.
elements.sent-2411-1253: Yet, locality is again recovered using the other (redundant) distance predicate $\ball(\bar y,x,r)$.
elements.sent-2411-1254: The second novelty of this paper is the implementation of quantifier elimination, including the new constraint of preserving locality, in \CONGEST, specifically in BROADCAST \CONGEST.
elements.sent-2411-1255: We had to tackle two main difficulties.
elements.sent-2411-1256: There are indeed operations that are very simple to perform in the centralized setting (they are even often implicit when described in the centralized algorithm), but it is not necessarily clear whether all these operations are local (i.e., involving nodes that are close in the graph), and frugal in term of bandwidth (i.e., requiring neighboring nodes to exchange a limited amount of information).
elements.sent-2411-1257: As a consequence, we had to carefully revisit the entire construction and 'open all boxes' to ensure that each step of the construction can be implemented efficiently in (BROADCAST) \CONGEST.
elements.sent-2411-1258: Second, model checking over graphs of bounded expansion actually refers to model checking of relational structures whose Gaifman graphs are of bounded expansion.
elements.sent-2411-1259: The techniques from \cite{PilipczukST18 (as well as other related approaches) involve enriching the structure relative to an arbitrary first-order formula with new symbols and predicates to obtain a quantifier-free formula.}
elements.sent-2411-1260: Enriching the structure naturally modifies Gaifman graphs.
elements.sent-2411-1261: When enriching the structure, one must therefore be very careful to avoid creating connections between nodes that cannot directly communicate in the network.
elements.subsubsection-2411-023: Model Checking, Counting, Optimization, and Certification
elements.sent-2411-1262: For checking whether $(G,\ell) \models \varphi$ in \CONGEST, for a given FO formula $\varphi$, and a given labeled graph $G\in\mathcal{G}$, we use the same techniques as the ones used to mark all nodes satisfying a given local FO formula.
elements.sent-2411-1263: That is, the proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:thmFOexpCONG follows the same guidelines as the ones used to establish Theorem~\ref{thm:thmFOlocal .}}
elements.sent-2411-1264: On the one hand, the approach is simplified as one does not need to care about relativization.
elements.sent-2411-1265: On the other hand, the "non-locality" of the formula requires checking properties that potentially occur at nodes that are far away from each other in the graph (e.g., the presence of at least two nodes of degree~3).
elements.sent-2411-1266: In the context of quantifier elimination, this translates into counting the number of nodes in which unary predicates are satisfied.
elements.sent-2411-1267: This counting is merely achieved along the edges of a breadth-first search (BFS) tree, in $\cO(D)$ rounds.
elements.sent-2411-1268: We show that all other operations involved in quantifier elimination can be performed in $\cO(\log n)$ rounds, for a total of $\cO(D+\log n)$ rounds.

elements.sent-2411-1270: For the global counting problem, we proceed in two stages.

elements.sent-2411-1287: Based on the above, it remains to deal with optimization and global counting for FO formulas on labeled subgraphs $G[U]$ of $G$, each of which has a constant treedepth.
elements.sent-2411-1288: For this purpose, we extend the results from~\cite{FominFMRT24 stating that counting and optimization of FO formulae can be achieved on connected labeled graphs of bounded treedepth, in a constant number of rounds.}
elements.sent-2411-1289: Indeed, we show that one can perform optimization and counting for FO formulae over an induced (not necessarily connected) subgraph of constant treedepth of the labeled graph~$G$, in $\cO(D)$ rounds.
elements.sent-2411-1290: If the input graph of small treedepth is connected, this directly enables the construction of a rooted tree decomposition of constant depth and constant width.
elements.sent-2411-1291: The root of the tree can then compute the output by simulating the centralized algorithm for optimization and counting on graphs of bounded treewidth of~\cite{BoPaTo92 .}
elements.sent-2411-1292: However, in our case, the graphs $G[U]$ may not be connected, and thus we must deal with a forest decomposition~$ F$ instead of a mere tree decomposition.
elements.sent-2411-1293: A BFS tree rooted at an arbitrary vertex can span all the roots of the trees in the forest~$F$, resulting in a rooted tree of depth $D + \cO(1)$.
elements.sent-2411-1294: This tree can serve as a base for constructing a tree-decomposition of $G[U]$.
elements.sent-2411-1295: Moreover, since the nodes of the tree-decomposition correspond to vertices of~$G$, the centralized dynamic programming algorithm of~\cite{BoPaTo92 can be simulated for counting and optimization on $G[U]$, in $D + \cO(1)$ rounds.}
elements.sent-2411-1296: Altogether, the whole process for counting and optimization for formula $\varphi$ on the pair $(G,\ell)$ costs $\cO(D + \log n)$ rounds in \CONGEST.

elements.sent-2411-1313: The results of our paper lie at the intersection of two research directions.
elements.sent-2411-1314: The first is the study of distributed computing and distributed certification schemes on sparse networks, such as planar graphs, or graphs excluding a fixed minor, for fundamental graph optimization problems.
elements.sent-2411-1315: The second is the development of meta-theorems for sparse graph classes in centralized computation.
elements.sent-2411-1316: One of the popular areas of research in distributed computing concerns the study of classical combinatorial problems on sparse networks, including planar graphs, bounded-degree graphs, graphs of bounded treewidth, and graphs excluding a fixed minor.
elements.sent-2411-1317: In such settings, a central goal is to develop distributed algorithms that exploit structural sparsity to achieve improved efficiency---compared to general networks---particularly in terms of communication rounds.
elements.sent-2411-1318: Typical problems studied in this context include coloring, matching, dominating sets, cuts, spanning trees, and network decompositions.
elements.sent-2411-1319: Research directions focus on understanding how sparsity affects the complexity of these problems in standard distributed models such as LOCAL and \CONGEST, and on designing algorithms with optimal or near-optimal round complexity.
elements.sent-2411-1320: This line of work often builds on structural properties of sparse networks, revealing deep and fruitful connections between structural graph theory and distributed computation.
elements.sent-2411-1321: Examples of the work in this direction include distributed algorithms for maximal matchings, maximal independent sets (MIS), and colourings in graphs of bounded vertex degrees \cite{panconesi2001simple , depth-first search (DFS) for planar graphs \cite{ghaffari:2017 , minimum spanning tree (MST) and min-cut for planar, bounded genus, and $H$-minor-free graphs \cite{GhaffariH16a,GhaffariH16b,haeupler2016low,HaeuplerIZ16,HaeuplerLZ18,GhaffariH21 , minimum dominating set on planar and $H$-minor-free graphs \cite{amiri2019distributed,bonamy2025local,czygrinow2008fast,czygrinow2018distributed,lenzen2013distributed , and MIS for graphs excluding a fixed minor \cite{chang2023efficient .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1322: The round complexity $\tilde{\mathcal{O (D)$ achieved in \Cref{thm:thmFOexpCONG , is considered a “gold standard” for algorithms on sparse graphs in the \CONGEST model.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1323: It has been obtained, for instance, for the Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) problem on planar graphs and, more generally, on $H$-minor-free graphs~\cite{GhaffariH16a,GhaffariH16b,haeupler2016low,HaeuplerIZ16,HaeuplerLZ18,GhaffariH21 .}
elements.sent-2411-1324: Algorithms with $\tilde{\mathcal{O (D)$ complexity in the \CONGEST model have also been developed for bounded-treewidth graphs for a variety of problems, including matching, the directed single-source shortest paths problem, bipartite unweighted maximum matching, and girth~\cite{izumi2022fully .}}}

elements.sent-2411-1348: Throughout the paper, we will use the following convention regarding the inputs and outputs of our algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-1349: When an algorithm receives a ``global value'' as input, whether it be an integer~$p$, a logical formula~$\varphi$, or a function (e.g., a bound $f:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ on the expansion), we assume that all nodes are given this global value as input.
elements.sent-2411-1350: Instead, when an algorithm receives as input a node labeling function~$\ell$, we assume that each node $v$ is only given its label $\ell(v)$.
elements.sent-2411-1351: Similarly, when an algorithm returns a node-labeling function $\widehat\ell$, we mean that each node $v$ returns its label $\widehat\ell(v)$.
elements.sent-2411-1352: A graph property is called a first-order (FO) property if it can be expressed by a first-order formula with no free variables.
elements.sent-2411-1353: For example, the property ``the graph contains a triangle'' is clearly an FO property: it holds whenever there is a set of three pairwise adjacent vertices in the graph.
elements.sent-2411-1354: Indeed, it can be written as the formula \begin{equation}\varphi \;=\; \exists x_1 \,\exists x_2 \,\exists x_3\, \bigl( adj(x_1,x_2) \;\wedge\;adj(x_2,x_3) \;\wedge\;adj(x_1,x_3) \bigr).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1355: More generally, the property of containing a fixed graph~\(H\) as a subgraph (sometimes called ``subgraph isomorphism'') is an FO property.
elements.sent-2411-1356: Its negation---namely, the property that a graph does not contain~\(H\) as a subgraph (often called ``\(H\)\!-freeness'')---is also an FO property, since first-order logic is closed under negation.

elements.sent-2411-1372: Local formulas can be defined as follows.
elements.sent-2411-1373: For a graph \(G = (V,E)\) and a vertex \(v\in V\), let \(B_G(v, r)\) denote the \(r\)-neighborhood of \(v\) in \(G\), that is, the subgraph of \(G\) induced by vertices at distance at most \(r\) from \(v\).

elements.sent-2411-1447: Graphs of bounded expansion~\cite{NesetrilOdM12 can be defined in term of local minors, and degeneracy, as pointed out in the Introduction.}
elements.sent-2411-1448: It is however more convenient for us to use the following characterization.
elements.sent-2411-1449: A class $\mathcal{G}$ of graphs has bounded expansion if there exists a function $f:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ such that, for every $G\in\mathcal{G}$, and for every $k\in \mathbb{N}$, there exists a coloring of the vertices of $G$ with colors in $[f(k)]=\{1,\dots,f(k)\}$ such that, for every set $U\subseteq [f(k)]$ of cardinality at most~$k$ the subgraph of~$G$ induced by the nodes colored by a color in~$U$ has treedepth at most~$|U|$.
elements.sent-2411-1450: For every positive integers $p$ and $k$, a $(p,k)$-treedepth coloring of a graph $G$ is a $k$-coloring of $G$ satisfying that each set $U\subseteq[k]$ of at most $p$ colors induces a graph of treedepth at most $|U|$.
elements.sent-2411-1451: A class of graphs has bounded expansion if it has expansion $f$ for some function~$f:\mathbb{N}\rightarrow \mathbb{N}$.
elements.sent-2411-1452: In the following, we always assume that the considered functions $f$ are Turing-computable.
elements.sent-2411-1453: Interestingly, treedepth colorings can be efficiently computed in the \CONGEST model.
elements.sent-2411-1454: There exists a function $g: \mathbb{N \to \mathbb{N}$ such that, for any graph $G \in \mathcal{G}$, and any constant positive integer~$p$, a $(p,g(p))$-treedepth coloring of $G$ can be computed in $\cO(\log n)$ rounds in the \CONGEST\ model.}
elements.sent-2411-1455: The algorithm of \cite{NesetrilM16 takes as input the function~$f$, and an integer~$p$, and outputs a $(p,g(p))$-treedepth coloring of the actual graph~$G$ in a distributed manner.}
elements.sent-2411-1456: The function $g$ may however be different from the given bound~$f$ on the expansion of the class~\(\mathcal G\), which may itself be different from the ``best'' expansion function for the class~\(\mathcal G\).

elements.sent-2411-1516: This formula can be rewritten as the $\lca$-reduced formula \begin{equation}\tilde{\varphi}(x_1, x_2, x_3) = \left( \bigvee_{i\in [0,d-2]} \xi^1_i(x_1,x_2,x_3)\right) \vee \left(\bigvee_{i\in [0,d-3]} \xi_i^2(x_1,x_2,x_3)\right) \vee \left(\bigvee_{i\in [0,d-3]} \xi_i^3(x_1,x_2,x_3) \right)\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\xi^1_i(x_1,x_2,x_3) = \bigg( \lca_i(x_1,x_1) \wedge \lca_{i+1}(x_2,x_2) \wedge \lca_{i+1}(x_3,x_3) \wedge \lca_i(x_1, x_2) \wedge \lca_i(x_1,x_3) \wedge \lca_i(x_2,x_3) \wedge \lab_1(x_1) \wedge \lab_0(x_2) \wedge \lab_0(x_3) \bigg),\end{equation} \begin{equation}\xi^2_i(x_1,x_2,x_3) = \bigg( \lca_{i+1}(x_1,x_1) \wedge \lca_i(x_2,x_2) \wedge \lca_{i+2}(x_3,x_3) \wedge \lca_i(x_1, x_2) \wedge \lca_{i+1}(x_1,x_3) \wedge \lca_i(x_2,x_3) \wedge \lab_1(x_1) \wedge \lab_0(x_2) \wedge \lab_0(x_3) \bigg),\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\xi^3_i(x_1,x_2,x_3) = \bigg( \lca_{i+1}(x_1,x_1)\wedge \lca_{i+2}(x_2,x_2) \wedge \lca_i(x_3,x_3) \wedge \lca_{i+1}(x_1, x_2) \wedge \lca_i(x_1,x_3) \wedge \lca_i(x_2,x_3)\wedge \lab_1(x_1) \wedge \lab_0(x_2) \wedge \lab_0(x_3) \bigg).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1517: Indeed, for every $i\in [0,d-2]$, $\xi_i^1$ is an $\lca$-type formula, and, for every $i\in [0,d-3]$, $\xi_i^2$ and $\xi_i^3$ are $\lca$-type formulas.
elements.sent-2411-1518: If a labeled forest $(F,\ell)$ with label set $\{0,1\}$ satisfies $\varphi$, then $F$ contains a forest isomorphic to $F_{\xi^1_i}$, $F_{\xi^2_i}$ or $F_{\xi^3_i}$, for some~$i\in [0,d-2]$.

elements.sent-2411-1519: In this section, we show that it is possible to eliminate existential quantifiers on $\lca$-expressed formulas, by augmenting the set of labels.
elements.sent-2411-1520: A formula $\varphi(\overline{x )\in \FO[\Lambda]$ is said reducible on $\mathcal{F_d[\Lambda]$} if there exists a set $\widehat{\Lambda}$, and an $\lca$-reduced formula $\widehat{\varphi (\overline{x ) \in \FO[\widehat{\Lambda ]$ such that (1)~$\widehat{\Lambda}$ and $\widehat{\varphi}$ depend only on $\Lambda$, $\varphi$, and~$d$, and (2)~for every $(F,\ell)\in \mathcal{F _d[\Lambda]$, there exists a $\widehat{\Lambda}$-labeling $\widehat{\ell}$ of $F$ such that \begin{equation}\true(\varphi,F,\ell) = \true(\widehat{\varphi},F,\widehat{\ell}).\end{equation}.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1521: Each element in the triplet $(\widehat{\Lambda ,\widehat{\varphi ,\widehat{\ell )$ of \Cref{def:reducibility is called the reduction on $\mathcal{F_d[\Lambda]$} of $\Lambda$, $\varphi$, and $\ell$, respectively.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-1524: A formula \(\varphi(\overline x) \in FO[\Lambda]\) is said \(r\)-local reducible on \(\mathcal F_d[\Lambda]\) if there exists a set $\widehat{\Lambda}$, and an $\lca$-reduced formula $\widehat{\varphi (\overline{x ) \in \FO[\widehat{\Lambda ]$ such that (1)~$\widehat{\Lambda}$ and $\widehat{\varphi}$ depend only on $\Lambda$, $\varphi$, and~$d$, and (2)~for every $(F,\ell)\in \mathcal{F _d[\Lambda]$, there exists a $\widehat{\Lambda}$-labeling $\widehat{\ell}$ of $F$ such that \begin{equation}\true(\varphi,F,\ell) = \true(\widehat{\varphi},F,\widehat{\ell}) = \true(\widehat\varphi\wedge\ball(\overline x,x_1,r), F, \widehat \ell).\end{equation}.}}}}

elements.subsection-2411-018: Quantifier Elimination on Graphs of Bounded Treedepth
elements.sent-2411-1616: We stress the fact that a decomposition forest $F$ of $G$ must be a subgraph of $G$, which is very convenient for distributed algorithms because this enables communication along the edges of~$F$.
elements.sent-2411-1617: Note that there exist graphs of treedepth~$t$ in which any decomposition forest is of diameter $\Omega(2^t)$.
elements.sent-2411-1618: This is for example the case of paths on $2^t-1$ vertices.
elements.sent-2411-1619: In particular, for any class of graphs of bounded treedepth, all possible decomposition forests also have trees of constant (although exponentially larger) diameter.
elements.sent-2411-1620: In the following lemma, we show that any distributed algorithm aiming at checking a quantifier-free formula in a treedepth-$t$ induced subgraph $H$ of a labeled graph~$G$, may rather check an equivalent formula on a decomposition forest of~$H$.

elements.sent-2411-1635: We now prove that existential quantifier in formulas on graphs of bounded treedepth can be eliminated efficiently.

elements.sent-2411-1637: Let \(\Lambda\) be a set of labels, \(\varphi(\overline x) = \exists \overline y, \zeta(\overline x, \overline y) \wedge \ball((\overline x, \overline y), x_1, r)\) be a \(r\)-local existential formula in \(\FO[\Lambda]\), where \(r\in \N\) and \(\zeta\) is a quantifier free formula.
elements.sent-2411-1638: There exists a set of labels~\(\widehat \Lambda\), and an \(\lca\)-reduced formula \(\widehat \varphi(\overline x)\) such that, for every treedepth-\(t\) induced subgraph \((H,\ell)\) of a \(\Lambda\)-labeled graph \((G,\ell)\), and for every decomposition forest \(F\) of \(H\), there exists a \(\widehat \Lambda\)-labeling \(\widehat \ell\) such that: \begin{equation}\true(\varphi, H, \ell) = \true(\widehat\varphi, F, \widehat \ell) = \true(\widehat\varphi \wedge\ball(\overline x, x_1,2^t), F, \widehat \ell).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1639: Moreover, there exists an algorithm computing \(\widehat \ell\) in \(\mathcal O(1)\) \CONGEST rounds, assuming each node \(v\) is given as input \(\ell(v)\), whether it belongs to \(F\) and, if applicable, its depth and parent in \(F\).

elements.sent-2411-1651: Let us fix a graph class $\mathcal{G}$ of expansion~$f$.

elements.sent-2411-1654: For every integer~$p$, let $\Col(p)$ be the collection of sets $U\subseteq[f(p)]$ with cardinality at most $p$.
elements.sent-2411-1655: We introduce a central notion for our proof of \Cref{thm:thmFOlocal .}
elements.sent-2411-1656: Let \(p \in \N\), and let $G$ be a graph of expansion~$f$.
elements.sent-2411-1657: A \(p\)-skeleton of \(G\) is a triple $S = (G, c, \delta)$ where \(c\) is \((p,f(p))\)-treedepth coloring of~\(G\), and $\delta$ is a function mapping every $U\in \Col(p)$ to a decomposition forest $F^U$ of the subgraph \(G[U]\) induced by nodes with colors in~\(U\).
elements.sent-2411-1658: Labeled \(p\)-skeletons are pairs \((S,\ell)\), where $S= (G, c, \delta)$ is a \(p\)-skeleton, and \(\ell\) is a labeling of \(G\).
elements.sent-2411-1659: Note that, by definition of a \((p,f(p))\)-treedepth coloring~$c$, the graph $G[U]$ is of treedepth at most~$p$ for every $U\in \Col(p)$.
elements.sent-2411-1661: The following lemma states that a $p$-skeleton can be computed in $\cO(\log n)$ rounds in the \CONGEST\ model, where the big-$\cO$ notation hides constants depending only of~$p$, and of the expansion of the class.
elements.sent-2411-1662: For every \(p \in \N\), there exists an algorithm that constructs a \(p\)-skeleton of \(G\) in \(\mathcal O(\log n)\) rounds in \CONGEST.
elements.sent-2411-1663: Specifically, at the end of the algorithm, each node knows its own color, and, for every set \(U\in\Col(p)\) containing its color, its parent and depth in the forest~\(F^U\) corresponding to the skeleton.

elements.sent-2411-1677: We now define a specific notion of quantifier elimination, denoted reducibility, for graphs of bounded expansion.
elements.sent-2411-1678: For this purpose, similarly to how FO formulas on treedepth-\(t\) graphs are transformed into FO formulas on their decomposition forests, we will transform FO formulas on graphs of \(\mathcal G\) into FO formulas on their \(p\)-skeletons.

elements.section-2411-009: Distributed Model Checking of General Formulas
elements.sent-2411-1788: We thus consider an arbitrary FO formulas --- not necessarily local.

elements.sent-2411-1790: Given a formula $\varphi(x)$ with one free variable, we provide a CONGEST algorithm that marks all nodes of $n$-node graphs with bounded expansion that satisfy the formula, in $\mathcal{O (D + \log n)$ rounds, where $D$ is the diameter of the input graph\footnote{This problem is more challenging than simply checking whether the graph satisfies a formula $\varphi$, i.e., stronger than the model checking problem as, given an FO formula $\varphi$ without free variables, one can define a logically equivalent formula $\varphi'(x)$ by adding a dummy free variable, so that the graph satisfies $\varphi$ if and only if it satisfies $\varphi'(v)$ at each of its nodes~$v$.. .}}

elements.sent-2411-2133: In this section we give evidence that our results somehow represent the limit of tractability of distributed model checking and distributed certification of properties on graphs of bounded expansion.
elements.sent-2411-2134: In the reduction each instance $(A,B)$ of $\Disj_{n^2}$ is associated with a $\cO(n)$-node graph $G_{A,B}$.
elements.sent-2411-2135: Each instance $A,B \in [m]$ is associated with a $\cO(n)$-node graph $G_{A,B}$ of degeneracy $2$.
elements.sent-2411-2136: Given an instance $A, B \in [m]$, the players construct $G_{A,B}$, simulate $\mathcal{A}$ in the nodes they own, and in each round communicate to the other player the messages sent from nodes on their side through $E_{A,B}$; in particular, the information communicated in one round is of size $\cO(n \log n)$.
elements.sent-2411-2137: In centralized model checking, the fixed-parameter tractability of FO was extended from graphs of bounded expansion to the more general class of nowhere dense graphs in~\cite{GroheKS14 .}
elements.sent-2411-2138: Notably, nowhere dense graphs represent the ultimate frontier in the program of designing fixed-parameter tractable algorithms for model checking FO formulas.
elements.sent-2411-2139: Indeed, as shown in~\cite{DvorakKT13 , for any graph class $\mathcal{G}$ that is not nowhere dense (i.e., $\mathcal{G}$ is somewhere dense) and closed under taking subgraphs, FO model checking on $\mathcal{G}$ is not fixed-parameter tractable (unless $\FPT = \W{1}$).}
elements.sent-2411-2140: The tractability frontier for FO model checking in the \CONGEST model remains unknown.
elements.sent-2411-2141: Moreover, there is no reason to expect that the tractability landscape in the distributed setting should mirror the one of the centralized setting.
elements.sent-2411-2142: This raises several concrete and intriguing questions, discussed hereafter.
elements.sent-2411-2143: Is it possible to design model-checking algorithms for FO formulas running in $\tilde{O (D)$ rounds in \CONGEST on nowhere dense graphs of diameter~$D$? From a theoretical perspective, a negative answer to this question would be even more insightful than a positive one.}
elements.sent-2411-2144: More broadly, what is the precise boundary for $\tilde{O (D)$-round FO model checking in the \CONGEST model? Does it coincide with the class of bounded expansion graphs, extend to nowhere dense graphs, or even include a larger family of graphs? There are several difficulties in extending our approach to nowhere dense graphs.}
elements.sent-2411-2145: First, it is unclear how to implement the combinatorial arguments from~\cite{GroheKS14 , particularly the constructions of sparse neighborhood covers, in a distributed model.}
elements.sent-2411-2146: Second, the proof of \cite{GroheKS14 builds on a weak variant of Gaifman's local form, and not a quantifier elimination procedure.}
elements.sent-2411-2147: Another question is whether a meta-theorem with $\tilde{O (D)$ round complexity can be obtained for some extensions of FO logic on planar graphs.}
elements.sent-2411-2148: On the other hand, in centralized computing, the fixed-parameter tractability of logics lying strictly between FO and MSO is an active area of research.
elements.sent-2411-2149: For example, the extension of FO known as separator logic, denoted $FOL+conn$, was introduced independently by Schirrmacher, Siebertz, and Vigny~\cite{schirrmacher2022first , and by Bojańczyk and Pilipczuk~\cite{bojanczyk2016definability .}}
elements.sent-2411-2150: This logic extends FO, for every $k \geq 1$, with the general predicate $\mathsf{conn _k(x, y, z_1, \ldots, z_k)$, which evaluates to true on a graph $G$ if the nodes corresponding to $x$ and $y$ are connected by a path that avoids the nodes corresponding to $\{z_1, \ldots, z_k\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-2151: Another example is disjoint paths logic, denoted $FOL+DP$, which extends FO with the atomic predicate $\mathsf{dp _k(x_1, y_1, \ldots, x_k, y_k)$, expressing the existence of $k$ pairwise internally vertex-disjoint paths between $x_i$ and $y_i$ for $i \in \{1, \ldots, k\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-2152: The main obstacle to developing distributed meta-theorems for these logics lies in the absence of distributed counterparts of the core algorithmic tools used in the centralized setting---such as tree decompositions which underlies tractability results for $FOL+conn$~\cite{pilipczuk2022algorithms , or the irrelevant vertex technique used for $FOL+DP$~\cite{golovach2023model .. \bibliographystyle{siam.}}}
